message.content: <local-command-caveat>Caveat: The messages below were generated by the user while running local commands. DO NOT respond to these messages or otherwise consider them in your response unless the user explicitly asks you to.</local-command-caveat>

content.text: Hey, can you pickup HARNESS_TODO

addedLines: - claude: Catch-all for any task that doesn't fit a more specific agent. FleetView's default when no agent name is typed. (Tools: *)

- claude-code-guide: Use this agent when the user asks questions ("Can Claude...", "Does Claude...", "How do I...") about: (1) Claude Code (the CLI tool) - features, hooks, slash commands, MCP servers, settings, IDE integrations, keyboard shortcuts; (2) Claude Agent SDK - building custom agents; (3) Claude API (formerly Anthropic API) - API usage, tool use, Anthropic SDK usage. **IMPORTANT:** Before spawning a new agent, check if there is already a running or recently completed claude-code-guide agent that you can continue via SendMessage. (Tools: Bash, Read, WebFetch, WebSearch)

- code-simplifier:code-simplifier: Simplifies and refines code for clarity, consistency, and maintainability while preserving all functionality. Focuses on recently modified code unless instructed otherwise. (Tools: All tools)

- docs-consistency-guard: Documentation agent that audits docs for staleness and inconsistency AND generates/updates documentation on request. Use proactively when reviewing any PR that touches CLAUDE.md, docs/adr/, CONTRIBUTING.md, or docs/architecture.md. Also invoke when the user asks "is the documentation up to date?", "update the milestone", "scaffold the ADR for X", "add this to the ADR index", "add a TODO item", "scaffold a README for X", or "create a doc stub for Y." (Tools: Read, Grep, Glob, Bash, Write, Edit)

- entire-search: Search Entire checkpoint history and transcripts with `entire search --json`. Use proactively when the user asks about previous work, commits, sessions, prompts, or historical context in this repository. (Tools: Bash)

- Explore: Read-only search agent for broad fan-out searches — when answering means sweeping many files, directories, or naming conventions and you only need the conclusion, not the file dumps. It reads excerpts rather than whole files, so it locates code; it doesn't review or audit it. Specify search breadth: "medium" for moderate exploration, "very thorough" for multiple locations and naming conventions. (Tools: All tools except Agent, Artifact, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)

- general-purpose: General-purpose agent for researching complex questions, searching for code, and executing multi-step tasks. When you are searching for a keyword or file and are not confident that you will find the right match in the first few tries use this agent to perform the search for you. (Tools: *)

- migration-safety: Specialized reviewer for Alembic migrations under backend/alembic/versions/. Audits for backward-incompatible operations that would break running code during a deploy, per working-agreement (Tools: Read, Grep, Glob, Bash)

- orchestration-abstraction-guard: Specialized reviewer that audits backend code for provider-specific branching that bypasses the OrchestrationProvider abstraction. Use this proactively when reviewing any PR that touches backend/app/orchestration/, backend/app/services/, or backend/app/api/ — especially any code path handling ADF or Airflow events, trigger bindings, or pipeline_runs. Also invoke when the user asks "does this go through the abstraction?" or similar. (Tools: Read, Grep, Glob, Bash)

- Plan: Software architect agent for designing implementation plans. Use this when you need to plan the implementation strategy for a task. Returns step-by-step plans, identifies critical files, and considers architectural trade-offs. (Tools: All tools except Agent, Artifact, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)

- pr-review-toolkit:code-reviewer: Use this agent when you need to review code for adherence to project guidelines, style guides, and best practices. This agent should be used proactively after writing or modifying code, especially before committing changes or creating pull requests. It will check for style violations, potential issues, and ensure code follows the established patterns in CLAUDE.md. Also the agent needs to know which files to focus on for the review. In most cases this will be recently completed work which is unstaged in git (can be retrieved by running git diff). However there can be cases where this is different, make sure to specify this as the agent input when calling the agent. Typical triggers include the user asking for a review of a feature they just implemented, the assistant proactively reviewing its own newly-written code before declaring a task done, and a final pre-PR check before opening a pull request. See "When to invoke" in the agent body for worked scenarios. (Tools: All tools)

- pr-review-toolkit:code-simplifier: Use this agent when code has been written or modified and needs to be simplified for clarity, consistency, and maintainability while preserving all functionality. This agent should be triggered automatically after completing a coding task or writing a logical chunk of code. It simplifies code by following project best practices while retaining all functionality. The agent focuses only on recently modified code unless instructed otherwise.

Examples:

<example>
Context: The assistant has just implemented a new feature that adds user authentication to an API endpoint.
user: "Please add authentication to the /api/users endpoint"
assistant: "I've implemented the authentication for the /api/users endpoint. Here's the code:"
<function call omitted for brevity>
<commentary>
Since a logical chunk of code has been written (authentication feature), use the Task tool to launch the code-simplifier agent to improve the code's clarity and maintainability while preserving functionality.
</commentary>
assistant: "Now let me use the code-simplifier agent to refine this implementation for better clarity and maintainability"
</example>

<example>
Context: The assistant has just fixed a bug by adding several conditional checks.
user: "Fix the null pointer exception in the data processor"
assistant: "I've added the necessary null checks to prevent the exception:"
<function call omitted for brevity>
<commentary>
After modifying code to fix a bug, use the code-simplifier agent to ensure the fix follows best practices and maintains code quality.
</commentary>
assistant: "Let me refine this bug fix using the code-simplifier agent to ensure it follows our best practices"
</example>

<example>
Context: The assistant has just refactored a function to improve performance.
user: "Optimize the data sorting algorithm for better performance"
assistant: "I've optimized the sorting algorithm. Here's the updated implementation:"
<function call omitted for brevity>
<commentary>
After completing a performance optimization task, use the code-simplifier agent to ensure the optimized code is also clear and maintainable.
</commentary>
assistant: "Now I'll use the code-simplifier agent to ensure the optimized code is also clear and follows our coding standards"
</example> (Tools: All tools)

- pr-review-toolkit:comment-analyzer: Use this agent when you need to analyze code comments for accuracy, completeness, and long-term maintainability. This includes (1) after generating large documentation comments or docstrings, (2) before finalizing a pull request that adds or modifies comments, (3) when reviewing existing comments for potential technical debt or comment rot, and (4) when you need to verify that comments accurately reflect the code they describe. See "When to invoke" in the agent body for worked scenarios. (Tools: All tools)

- pr-review-toolkit:pr-test-analyzer: Use this agent when you need to review a pull request for test coverage quality and completeness. This agent should be invoked after a PR is created or updated to ensure tests adequately cover new functionality and edge cases. Typical triggers include the user asking whether tests on a freshly-created PR are thorough, an updated PR adding new logic that needs coverage analysis, and a final pre-merge double-check before marking a PR ready. See "When to invoke" in the agent body for worked scenarios. (Tools: All tools)

- pr-review-toolkit:silent-failure-hunter: Use this agent when reviewing code changes in a pull request to identify silent failures, inadequate error handling, and inappropriate fallback behavior. This agent should be invoked proactively after completing a logical chunk of work that involves error handling, catch blocks, fallback logic, or any code that could potentially suppress errors. Examples:

<example>
Context: Daisy has just finished implementing a new feature that fetches data from an API with fallback behavior.
Daisy: "I've added error handling to the API client. Can you review it?"
Assistant: "Let me use the silent-failure-hunter agent to thoroughly examine the error handling in your changes."
<Task tool invocation to launch silent-failure-hunter agent>
</example>

<example>
Context: Daisy has created a PR with changes that include try-catch blocks.
Daisy: "Please review PR #1234"
Assistant: "I'll use the silent-failure-hunter agent to check for any silent failures or inadequate error handling in this PR."
<Task tool invocation to launch silent-failure-hunter agent>
</example>

<example>
Context: Daisy has just refactored error handling code.
Daisy: "I've updated the error handling in the authentication module"
Assistant: "Let me proactively use the silent-failure-hunter agent to ensure the error handling changes don't introduce silent failures."
<Task tool invocation to launch silent-failure-hunter agent>
</example> (Tools: All tools)

- pr-review-toolkit:type-design-analyzer: Use this agent when you need expert analysis of type design in your codebase. Specifically use it (1) when introducing a new type to ensure it follows best practices for encapsulation and invariant expression, (2) during pull request creation to review all types being added, and (3) when refactoring existing types to improve their design quality. The agent will provide both qualitative feedback and quantitative ratings on encapsulation, invariant expression, usefulness, and enforcement. See "When to invoke" in the agent body for worked scenarios. (Tools: All tools)
- statusline-setup: Use this agent to configure the user's Claude Code status line setting. (Tools: Read, Edit)

## claude.ai Context7
Use this server to fetch current documentation whenever the user asks about a library, framework, SDK, API, CLI tool, or cloud service -- even well-known ones like React, Next.js, Prisma, Express, Tailwind, Django, or Spring Boot. This includes API syntax, configuration, version migration, library-specific debugging, setup instructions, and CLI tool usage. Use even when you think you know the answer -- your training data may not reflect recent changes. Prefer this over web search for library docs.

Do not use for: refactoring, writing scripts from scratch, debugging business logic, code review, or general programming concepts.

## claude.ai Hugging Face
You have tools for using the Hugging Face Hub. arXiv paper id's are often used as references between datasets, models and papers. There are over 100 tags in use, common tags include 'Text Generation', 'Transformers', 'Image Classification' and so on.
Hugging Face tools are being used by authenticated user 'TheurgicDuke771'

## claude.ai Microsoft Learn
# Microsoft Learn MCP Server

This server gives structured access to official Microsoft and Azure documentation via three tools:

## Tools

### microsoft_docs_search
Search official documentation and return up to 10 concise, high-quality content chunks (max 500 tokens each), including title, URL, and excerpt.

- Use first to get a quick, reliable overview
- Ideal for grounding answers in Microsoft knowledge

### microsoft_code_sample_search
Search for code snippets and examples in official Microsoft Learn documentation and return up to 20 relevant, high-quality code samples.

- Use when you need to provide sample Microsoft/Azure related code in your answers.  
- Ideal for generating code snippets or practical implementation examples.  
- Optional parameter `language` can filter results. 

### microsoft_docs_fetch
Fetch and convert full Microsoft documentation pages to markdown.

- Use after search when you need full content from a specific URL
- Required for detailed tutorials, troubleshooting, prerequisites, code samples, or when search results are incomplete or outdated

## Workflow

1. Use `microsoft_docs_search` to find relevant documents.
2. If you need code examples or practical snippets, use `microsoft_code_sample_search`.
3. If deeper or complete information is needed, use `microsoft_docs_fetch`.

**Search gives breadth. Code Sample Search gives practical examples. Fetch gives depth.**

All content comes from Microsoft Learn or official sources, returned in clean markdown format.

## claude.ai Postman
Read the instructions resource completely for detailed usage instructions before answering any API-related questions.

## context7
Use this server to retrieve up-to-date documentation and code examples for any library.

attachment.content:
- commit-commands:clean_gone: Cleans up all git branches marked as [gone] (branches that have been deleted on the remote but still exist locally), including removing associated worktrees.
- commit-commands:commit-push-pr: Commit, push, and open a PR
- commit-commands:commit: Create a git commit
- code-review:code-review: Code review a pull request
- pr-review-toolkit:review-pr: Comprehensive PR review using specialized agents
- claude-code-setup:claude-automation-recommender: Analyze a codebase and recommend Claude Code automations (hooks, subagents, skills, plugins, MCP servers). Use when user asks for automation recommendations, wants to optimize their Claude Code setup, mentions improving Claude Code workflows, asks how to first set up Claude Code for a project, or wants to know what Claude Code features they should use.
- frontend-design:frontend-design: Guidance for distinctive, intentional visual design when building new UI or reshaping an existing one. Helps with aesthetic direction, typography, and making choices that don't read as templated defaults.
- skill-creator:skill-creator: Create new skills, modify and improve existing skills, and measure skill performance. Use when users want to create a skill from scratch, edit, or optimize an existing skill, run evals to test a skill, benchmark skill performance with variance analysis, or optimize a skill's description for better triggering accuracy.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.content:
1	# DataQ — Live test/demo-data environment · local tracker
2	
3	> **NOT git-tracked** (matched by `.gitignore` → `*_TODO.md`). This is the working tracker for the
4	> external harness described in **ADR 0021** (`docs/adr/0021-demo-test-data-environment-strategy.md`).
5	> The harness code itself (Terraform, mock-data generators, Databricks notebook) also lives **outside**
6	> the DataQ repo — keep paths/links here pointing at wherever you stand that up.
7	>
8	> Purpose: discharge the standing *"deferred live warehouse/file run smoke"* caveat with a real,
9	> repeatable retail data environment exercising all three DataQ run paths + the ADF/Airflow trigger path.
10	>
11	> Legend: `[ ]` todo · `[~]` in progress · `[x]` done · `[-]` skipped/deferred
12	
13	Suggested order: **0 → 1 → 2 → (3a/3b/3c in parallel) → 4 → 5 → 6** (perf rides §2 volume + §3 run paths).
14	Harness root: `~/Coding/Python/DataQ-harness/` · secrets: `secrets.sh` (chmod 600, never committed)
15	
16	---
17	
18	## 0. Prerequisites (accounts + local tooling)
19	
20	- [x] Azure account/subscription enabled; resource group `dataq-rg` created in West US 2 (sub: `Azure subscription 1`)
21	- [x] Snowflake account enabled — `PVQSOEQ-ZGB34383` (AWS us-west-2, Standard); `snow connection test` OK; creds in `secrets.sh` + `~/.snowflake/connections.toml` (chmod 600)
22	- [x] Databricks workspace enabled — express setup (AWS us-east-2); Unity Catalog metastore `metastore_aws_us_east_2` auto-provisioned; PAT `dapi...` in `secrets.sh`; host `https://dbc-b16e0901-d85c.cloud.databricks.com`; workspace admin confirmed via SCIM API
23	- [x] Local tooling: `terraform` 1.15.7, `az` CLI 2.87.0, `snowflake-cli` (`snow` 3.21.0), Docker — all installed
24	- [x] Secret handling decided: `secrets.sh` in harness root (chmod 600, sourced before terraform/snow commands, never committed); Terraform SP `dataq-terraform-sp` created with Contributor on `dataq-rg`
25	
26	---
27	
28	## 1. Terraform — cloud infra bootstrap (external, not git-tracked)
29	
30	> Provisions the **datasources** (ADLS / Snowflake / UC). Airflow is **local Docker** (§3), so Terraform
31	> does NOT provision Airflow in the v1 profile. ADF is used in its managed cloud form.
32	
33	- [ ] `terraform/` skeleton in the harness root with backend + provider blocks (azurerm, snowflake, databricks)
34	- [ ] **ADLS Gen2**: storage account + container(s) for landing zone (e.g. `landing/`, `raw/`)
35	- [ ] **Snowflake**: database + schema(s) + warehouse + a least-privilege role/user for DataQ to read
36	- [ ] **Unity Catalog**: catalog + RAW/SILVER/GOLD schemas; grants for the DataQ PAT
37	- [ ] **ADF**: data factory instance (for Flow A's load pipelines)
38	- [ ] Outputs: connection coordinates DataQ's connection editor needs (account URLs, warehouse ids, paths)
39	- [ ] `terraform apply` clean; record outputs into the harness `.env` (local only)
40	- [ ] Teardown verified (`terraform destroy`) so the demo env is disposable
41	- [ ] **No Azure resource names leak into the DataQ repo** (ADR 0010/0013) — they live only here
42	
43	---
44	
45	## 2. Retail data model + mock-data generators (external harness)
46	
47	> Domains → entities to generate. Believable retail data so checks/profiling/severity/trends look real.
48	
49	- [ ] **Product** — style, colour, material, SKU
50	- [ ] **Inventory** — on-hand stock, planned
51	- [ ] **Location** — store, address, store operating hours
52	- [ ] **Orders** — split **header** (order_number, channel, guest, status, order_total, tax, timestamps) ↔ **line items** (one row per SKU: qty, unit_price, line_total). Most checks run at line grain (`qty > 0`, `unit_price`, `line_total = qty × unit_price`); it's where `sku_id` joins Product; and `sum(line_total) = order_total` is an **intra-dataset reconciliation**
53	- [ ] **Sales lifecycle** — placed · shipped · audited events (each carries `order_number`)
54	- [ ] **Payments / Finance** — payment (method, status, captured amount), refund, settlement ledger — the reconciliation testbed: `order_total ↔ payment_captured ↔ refund` (feeds the reserved `comparison` kind, ADR 0014)
55	- [ ] **Return / Cancellation** — return, cancellation, guest, channel
56	- [ ] **Logistics** — shipment, tracking, carrier (+ fulfilling node)
57	- [ ] **Channel / Marketplace** — online · store · marketplace as a **first-class dimension** (a common DQ slice axis: null channel, orphan marketplace) — not just a `platform` column buried in returns
58	- [ ] **Guest / Customer** — guest details (name, address, **email, phone** — the PII targets for sample-row redaction #226), survey data, chatbot/feedback
59	- [ ] **Cross-cutting** — on-sale/discount, promotion/award/coupon, tax rate, exchange rate, product price, misc
60	- [ ] _Optional (domain realism, lower priority — add if a check needs them):_
61	  - **Supplier / Vendor + Purchase Orders** — inbound supply → inventory replenishment (freshness on inbound feeds)
62	  - **Fulfillment node / DC / warehouse** — the location that fulfilled an order (a join target beyond the store `Location`)
63	  - **Catalog hierarchy** — category · department · brand above Product (orphan / FK-integrity checks)
64	  - **Calendar / date dimension** — fiscal week · season for trend slicing
65	
66	**Route map — which dataset lands on which DataQ run path** (decided; per-flow detail in §3):
67	
68	| Dataset | Route | Platform · format / layer |
69	|---|---|---|
70	| Logistics **tracking events** | Flow C — flat-file in place | ADLS · **CSV** · batch-resolved (latest) |
71	| **Inventory daily snapshot** | Flow C — flat-file in place | ADLS · **Parquet** · latest-snapshot |
72	| **Orders** (header+line) · **Payments/Finance** · **Returns/Cancellations** · **Customer** master · **Product/SKU** master | Flow A — load to warehouse | **Snowflake** (SQL-heavy: custom-SQL, line-grain, reconciliation) |
73	| **Location** · **Promotions/pricing/coupons** · **Channel/Marketplace** | Flow A — load to warehouse | **Unity Catalog** (plain load — proves the UC runner *without* medallion) |
74	| **Feedback/survey/chatbot** · **Sales → revenue marts** | Flow B — medallion | **Databricks** bronze → silver → gold (UC) |
75	
76	> **Discipline — one system-of-record per entity.** Snowflake `Orders` is the truth; the medallion `gold` revenue is a *derived* aggregate that must reconcile **back** to it (Snowflake `order_total` ↔ Databricks `gold.daily_revenue` — the ADR 0014 `comparison` demo). Never author the same entity authoritatively in two places, or the reconciliation / FK checks measure generation noise instead of real DQ — the same guard as the key-consistency rule above.
77	>
78	> **Reserved-kind homes:** medallion **gold** tables host freshness/volume/anomaly monitors (ADR 0012 — daily aggregates that should refresh); the flat-file feeds host freshness + batch-resolution; the Snowflake↔Databricks pair hosts the cross-platform `comparison`.
79	
80	- [ ] Generator script (Python — Faker or similar) emitting CSV/Parquet to the ADLS landing zone
81	- [ ] **Keep shared keys consistent across domains AND across batch/incremental loads** — figure out a generation strategy (shared key pools / referential seeding) so the same identifier means the same entity everywhere:
82	  - `order_number` is identical across **Orders · Sales · Return · Logistics**
83	  - `sku_id` is identical across **Product · Inventory · Orders**
84	  - a shipment keeps **one `tracking_number`** across its `Ready For Shipment` → `In Transit` → `Delivered` events — and that number stays stable across different batches / data loads (events arrive incrementally)
85	  - _Why:_ without referential consistency the cross-dataset reconciliation / FK-orphan / join-based checks are meaningless (everything looks broken); the deliberate DQ issues below must be the **only** inconsistencies, not generation noise.
86	- [ ] **Shape ≥1 dataset for each reserved monitor kind** (ADR 0012 — "most real incidents are freshness/volume, not value-level; this is the leap from a GX runner to a DQ platform") so the seam has a live target when those kinds are built:
87	  - **Freshness · Volume · Anomaly** — make one feed **timestamped + incrementally loaded** (e.g. an hourly orders feed or a daily inventory snapshot with a `load_ts`): a freshness check ("last load < 1h"), a volume band ("rows within ±20%"), and an anomaly (sudden volume drop) each bite on it
88	  - **Schema drift** — one feed whose columns you can perturb across loads (add / drop / rename a column, change a type)
89	  - **Comparison / reconciliation** — the two source-vs-target pairs above (`line_total → order_total`; `order_total ↔ payment ↔ refund`) as the FDC-style diff target (ADR 0014; reserved now, built post-v1, but shape the data now)
90	  - _v1 only authors GX `expectation` checks — these kinds are **reserved** (ADR 0012); shaping the data now means the harness is ready the day they ship, no regen needed._
91	- [ ] **Seed some deliberate DQ issues** (nulls, dupes, FK orphans, stale dates, volume drops, a schema change, a reconciliation mismatch) so checks actually fail — proves severity tiers + alerting, not just green dashboards
92	- [ ] Parameterise volume + a `--seed` for reproducibility; document run command in harness README
93	
94	---
95	
96	## 3. Reference flows (each exercises a distinct DataQ run path)
97	
98	### 3a. Flow A — load to warehouse  →  `SnowflakeCheckRunner` / `UnityCatalogCheckRunner`
99	
100	> **Datasets:** **Snowflake** ← Orders (header+line) · Payments/Finance · Returns/Cancellations · Customer master · Product/SKU master (transactional + financial core; custom-SQL + line-grain + `sum(line_total)=order_total` + `order_total↔payment↔refund` reconciliation). **Unity Catalog (plain load)** ← Location · Promotions/pricing/coupons · Channel/Marketplace (governed reference data — proves the UC runner *without* the medallion machinery).
101	
102	- [ ] File lands in ADLS landing zone (from §2 generator)
103	- [ ] **ADF pipeline** (or Airflow DAG) picks the file up and loads it into Snowflake / UC
104	- [ ] On success, the orchestrator hits DataQ's trigger path (ADF webhook / Airflow callback → trigger binding)
105	- [ ] DataQ suite runs against the loaded **warehouse table**; result row persists
106	- [ ] Verify in `/results`: run succeeded, severity derived, metric_value populated
107	- [ ] **Snowflake suite** exercises a **custom-SQL** check (ADR 0019) — e.g. the `sum(line_total)=order_total` / `order_total↔payment↔refund` aggregate — not just column expectations
108	- [ ] **UC plain-load suite** runs on a Location / Promotions table (no Silver/Gold transform — that's Flow B)
109	
110	### 3b. Flow B — medallion  →  UC tables
111	
112	> **Datasets:** **Feedback/survey/chatbot** (bronze raw JSON/text → silver cleaned + **PII-handled** [#226] → gold sentiment/NPS feature table — the lakehouse/ML "feature store" story) and **Sales → revenue marts** (bronze raw sales events → silver cleaned daily sales by store·channel → gold revenue/units rollups).
113	
114	- [ ] File lands in **UC RAW (bronze)** layer
115	- [ ] **Databricks notebook** (external, not git-tracked) runs: bronze → silver → gold transforms
116	- [ ] DataQ suites run against the UC Silver/Gold tables
117	- [ ] **Gold tables host the reserved freshness/volume/anomaly targets** (ADR 0012 — daily aggregates that should refresh; "did the rollup load / did volume drop")
118	- [ ] **Cross-platform reconciliation target** (ADR 0014): Snowflake `Orders.order_total` ↔ Databricks `gold.daily_revenue` should tie out (the `comparison` demo; built post-v1, shaped now)
119	- [ ] (Optional) notebook completion triggers the DataQ run via Airflow/ADF binding
120	- [ ] Verify results per layer (bronze schema-drift, silver PII-redaction, gold freshness/volume)
121	
122	### 3c. Flow C — flat-file in place  →  `FlatFileCheckRunner` + batch resolution
123	
124	> **Datasets:** **Logistics tracking events** (ADLS · **CSV** · event-append — `tracking_number` stable across `Ready For Shipment → In Transit → Delivered` batches) and **Inventory daily snapshot** (ADLS · **Parquet** · daily-snapshot — exercises the zero-copy Arrow read, PR #140). One of each format × each batch shape (event-append vs daily-snapshot).
125	
126	- [ ] File lands in ADLS and **stays a flat file** (no warehouse load)
127	- [ ] DataQ flat-file connection + suite targets the path with a **batching regex**
128	- [ ] Run resolves the **latest** batch and checks it directly
129	- [ ] Verify batch resolution picks the right file + result persists
130	- [ ] **CSV** path: tracking events — assert the tracking-number / event-status checks + freshness ("last drop < 1h")
131	- [ ] **Parquet** path: inventory snapshot — assert volume band (±20%) + freshness on the latest snapshot
132	
133	---
134	
135	## 4. QA/QE agent — module-by-module + E2E (this IS git-tracked: `.claude/agents/`)
136	
137	> The one in-repo deliverable. A Claude Code subagent, sibling to `migration-safety` /
138	> `orchestration-abstraction-guard`. Lands in Week 8 / ongoing.
139	
140	- [ ] Define agent scope: per-module functional checks (connections, suites, checks, run paths, profiler, results, orchestration) + full E2E against the harness
141	- [ ] Write `.claude/agents/qa-qe.md` (tools, model, system prompt)
142	- [ ] Wire it to drive the three flows above as E2E smoke once the harness is up
143	- [ ] Decide invocation cadence (per-PR? on-demand? pre-release?)
144	- [ ] **This file is committed** — open a PR per working agreements (branch `feature/qa-qe-agent`)
145	
146	---
147	
148	## 5. HashiCorp Vault — secret-management spike (post-v1 / backlog)
149	
150	> Validates the `SecretStore` seam (ADR 0010/0013): Azure Key Vault is **one** impl, not the architecture.
151	
152	- [ ] Stand up Vault locally (dev mode / Docker) in the harness
153	- [ ] Prototype a `VaultSecretStore` against the existing `SecretStore` interface (spike branch, not merged)
154	- [ ] Confirm no interface change needed (if it needs one, that's a finding worth an ADR note)
155	- [ ] Write up: does the seam hold? effort to make Vault a first-class option? → backlog issue / ADR follow-up
156	
157	---
158	
159	## 6. Performance / scale testing (post-v1 / backlog)
160	
161	> Rides §2's parameterizable volume (`--seed` + a `--volume` knob): generate **S / M / L / XL** of the same
162	> datasets, run, measure, record. **Baseline-first** — establish numbers + a regression budget; no hard SLAs
163	> yet (set them once we know where the knees are). Standalone harness script; the **QA/QE agent (§4)** can
164	> invoke it later as a perf-smoke. Needs §2 (generators) + §3 (the run paths) green first.
165	
166	**Lead axes (do these first):**
167	- [ ] **Run scaling** *(headline)* — GX run wall-clock vs **rows × #checks-per-suite**, per datasource (Snowflake · UC · flat-file CSV+Parquet). Capture throughput (rows/sec) + **worker memory peak** (GX pulls the batch in-memory) — and note whether time is in the warehouse query or GX itself.
168	- [ ] **Profiler latency** — profile a **wide** table (many columns) to quantify the known **N+1 per-column top-values** cost ([#327](https://github.com/TheurgicDuke771/DataQ/issues/327)) and the payoff once it's batched.
169	- [ ] **DB growth** — p95 latency of `/results`, `/dashboard/summary`, `/runs` as `results`/`runs` grow to 100k+ rows; validates the retention-sweep index ([#323](https://github.com/TheurgicDuke771/DataQ/issues/323)).
170	
171	**Secondary axes:**
172	- [ ] **Concurrency / throughput** — N suites at once: Celery pool saturation, the 60s scheduler beat under many due schedules (`FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED`), run-now storms → queue wait time.
173	- [ ] **Flat-file batch resolution at scale** — resolving the latest batch among a large landing zone (thousands of files) — the list+regex cost.
174	
175	**Method:**
176	- [ ] One script: param = datasource × volume tier × #checks; emits a CSV/JSON of (metric, value) per run
177	- [ ] Record a **baseline table** (commit it with the QA/QE agent in §4) + a **regression budget** (e.g. fail if a tier regresses > X% vs baseline) — so perf regressions are caught, not just measured once
178	- [ ] Capture both **app** metrics (run duration, profiler latency, API p95) and **resource** metrics (worker mem peak, DB CPU)
179	- [ ] Feed findings back: confirm/close #327 (profiler) + #323 (retention index); file new issues for any knee found
180	
181	---
182	
183	## Tracking notes
184	
185	- [ ] File the milestoned GitHub issues (per convention): Terraform → W7 · mock-data + Flows A/B/C → W7 · QA/QE agent → W8 · Vault → "Backlog (post-v1 / testing)" · perf/scale testing → "Backlog (post-v1 / testing)"  *(currently NOT filed — pending decision)*
186	- [ ] When a flow first runs green end-to-end, update `docs/progress.md` (flip the ⬜ in the workstream table) and note the live smoke is discharged in CLAUDE.md §13

file.content:
# DataQ — Live test/demo-data environment · local tracker

> **NOT git-tracked** (matched by `.gitignore` → `*_TODO.md`). This is the working tracker for the
> external harness described in **ADR 0021** (`docs/adr/0021-demo-test-data-environment-strategy.md`).
> The harness code itself (Terraform, mock-data generators, Databricks notebook) also lives **outside**
> the DataQ repo — keep paths/links here pointing at wherever you stand that up.
>
> Purpose: discharge the standing *"deferred live warehouse/file run smoke"* caveat with a real,
> repeatable retail data environment exercising all three DataQ run paths + the ADF/Airflow trigger path.
>
> Legend: `[ ]` todo · `[~]` in progress · `[x]` done · `[-]` skipped/deferred

Suggested order: **0 → 1 → 2 → (3a/3b/3c in parallel) → 4 → 5 → 6** (perf rides §2 volume + §3 run paths).
Harness root: `~/Coding/Python/DataQ-harness/` · secrets: `secrets.sh` (chmod 600, never committed)

---

## 0. Prerequisites (accounts + local tooling)

- [x] Azure account/subscription enabled; resource group `dataq-rg` created in West US 2 (sub: `Azure subscription 1`)
- [x] Snowflake account enabled — `PVQSOEQ-ZGB34383` (AWS us-west-2, Standard); `snow connection test` OK; creds in `secrets.sh` + `~/.snowflake/connections.toml` (chmod 600)
- [x] Databricks workspace enabled — express setup (AWS us-east-2); Unity Catalog metastore `metastore_aws_us_east_2` auto-provisioned; PAT `dapi...` in `secrets.sh`; host `https://dbc-b16e0901-d85c.cloud.databricks.com`; workspace admin confirmed via SCIM API
- [x] Local tooling: `terraform` 1.15.7, `az` CLI 2.87.0, `snowflake-cli` (`snow` 3.21.0), Docker — all installed
- [x] Secret handling decided: `secrets.sh` in harness root (chmod 600, sourced before terraform/snow commands, never committed); Terraform SP `dataq-terraform-sp` created with Contributor on `dataq-rg`

---

## 1. Terraform — cloud infra bootstrap (external, not git-tracked)

> Provisions the **datasources** (ADLS / Snowflake / UC). Airflow is **local Docker** (§3), so Terraform
> does NOT provision Airflow in the v1 profile. ADF is used in its managed cloud form.

- [ ] `terraform/` skeleton in the harness root with backend + provider blocks (azurerm, snowflake, databricks)
- [ ] **ADLS Gen2**: storage account + container(s) for landing zone (e.g. `landing/`, `raw/`)
- [ ] **Snowflake**: database + schema(s) + warehouse + a least-privilege role/user for DataQ to read
- [ ] **Unity Catalog**: catalog + RAW/SILVER/GOLD schemas; grants for the DataQ PAT
- [ ] **ADF**: data factory instance (for Flow A's load pipelines)
- [ ] Outputs: connection coordinates DataQ's connection editor needs (account URLs, warehouse ids, paths)
- [ ] `terraform apply` clean; record outputs into the harness `.env` (local only)
- [ ] Teardown verified (`terraform destroy`) so the demo env is disposable
- [ ] **No Azure resource names leak into the DataQ repo** (ADR 0010/0013) — they live only here

---

## 2. Retail data model + mock-data generators (external harness)

> Domains → entities to generate. Believable retail data so checks/profiling/severity/trends look real.

- [ ] **Product** — style, colour, material, SKU
- [ ] **Inventory** — on-hand stock, planned
- [ ] **Location** — store, address, store operating hours
- [ ] **Orders** — split **header** (order_number, channel, guest, status, order_total, tax, timestamps) ↔ **line items** (one row per SKU: qty, unit_price, line_total). Most checks run at line grain (`qty > 0`, `unit_price`, `line_total = qty × unit_price`); it's where `sku_id` joins Product; and `sum(line_total) = order_total` is an **intra-dataset reconciliation**
- [ ] **Sales lifecycle** — placed · shipped · audited events (each carries `order_number`)
- [ ] **Payments / Finance** — payment (method, status, captured amount), refund, settlement ledger — the reconciliation testbed: `order_total ↔ payment_captured ↔ refund` (feeds the reserved `comparison` kind, ADR 0014)
- [ ] **Return / Cancellation** — return, cancellation, guest, channel
- [ ] **Logistics** — shipment, tracking, carrier (+ fulfilling node)
- [ ] **Channel / Marketplace** — online · store · marketplace as a **first-class dimension** (a common DQ slice axis: null channel, orphan marketplace) — not just a `platform` column buried in returns
- [ ] **Guest / Customer** — guest details (name, address, **email, phone** — the PII targets for sample-row redaction #226), survey data, chatbot/feedback
- [ ] **Cross-cutting** — on-sale/discount, promotion/award/coupon, tax rate, exchange rate, product price, misc
- [ ] _Optional (domain realism, lower priority — add if a check needs them):_
  - **Supplier / Vendor + Purchase Orders** — inbound supply → inventory replenishment (freshness on inbound feeds)
  - **Fulfillment node / DC / warehouse** — the location that fulfilled an order (a join target beyond the store `Location`)
  - **Catalog hierarchy** — category · department · brand above Product (orphan / FK-integrity checks)
  - **Calendar / date dimension** — fiscal week · season for trend slicing

**Route map — which dataset lands on which DataQ run path** (decided; per-flow detail in §3):

| Dataset | Route | Platform · format / layer |
|---|---|---|
| Logistics **tracking events** | Flow C — flat-file in place | ADLS · **CSV** · batch-resolved (latest) |
| **Inventory daily snapshot** | Flow C — flat-file in place | ADLS · **Parquet** · latest-snapshot |
| **Orders** (header+line) · **Payments/Finance** · **Returns/Cancellations** · **Customer** master · **Product/SKU** master | Flow A — load to warehouse | **Snowflake** (SQL-heavy: custom-SQL, line-grain, reconciliation) |
| **Location** · **Promotions/pricing/coupons** · **Channel/Marketplace** | Flow A — load to warehouse | **Unity Catalog** (plain load — proves the UC runner *without* medallion) |
| **Feedback/survey/chatbot** · **Sales → revenue marts** | Flow B — medallion | **Databricks** bronze → silver → gold (UC) |

> **Discipline — one system-of-record per entity.** Snowflake `Orders` is the truth; the medallion `gold` revenue is a *derived* aggregate that must reconcile **back** to it (Snowflake `order_total` ↔ Databricks `gold.daily_revenue` — the ADR 0014 `comparison` demo). Never author the same entity authoritatively in two places, or the reconciliation / FK checks measure generation noise instead of real DQ — the same guard as the key-consistency rule above.
>
> **Reserved-kind homes:** medallion **gold** tables host freshness/volume/anomaly monitors (ADR 0012 — daily aggregates that should refresh); the flat-file feeds host freshness + batch-resolution; the Snowflake↔Databricks pair hosts the cross-platform `comparison`.

- [ ] Generator script (Python — Faker or similar) emitting CSV/Parquet to the ADLS landing zone
- [ ] **Keep shared keys consistent across domains AND across batch/incremental loads** — figure out a generation strategy (shared key pools / referential seeding) so the same identifier means the same entity everywhere:
  - `order_number` is identical across **Orders · Sales · Return · Logistics**
  - `sku_id` is identical across **Product · Inventory · Orders**
  - a shipment keeps **one `tracking_number`** across its `Ready For Shipment` → `In Transit` → `Delivered` events — and that number stays stable across different batches / data loads (events arrive incrementally)
  - _Why:_ without referential consistency the cross-dataset reconciliation / FK-orphan / join-based checks are meaningless (everything looks broken); the deliberate DQ issues below must be the **only** inconsistencies, not generation noise.
- [ ] **Shape ≥1 dataset for each reserved monitor kind** (ADR 0012 — "most real incidents are freshness/volume, not value-level; this is the leap from a GX runner to a DQ platform") so the seam has a live target when those kinds are built:
  - **Freshness · Volume · Anomaly** — make one feed **timestamped + incrementally loaded** (e.g. an hourly orders feed or a daily inventory snapshot with a `load_ts`): a freshness check ("last load < 1h"), a volume band ("rows within ±20%"), and an anomaly (sudden volume drop) each bite on it
  - **Schema drift** — one feed whose columns you can perturb across loads (add / drop / rename a column, change a type)
  - **Comparison / reconciliation** — the two source-vs-target pairs above (`line_total → order_total`; `order_total ↔ payment ↔ refund`) as the FDC-style diff target (ADR 0014; reserved now, built post-v1, but shape the data now)
  - _v1 only authors GX `expectation` checks — these kinds are **reserved** (ADR 0012); shaping the data now means the harness is ready the day they ship, no regen needed._
- [ ] **Seed some deliberate DQ issues** (nulls, dupes, FK orphans, stale dates, volume drops, a schema change, a reconciliation mismatch) so checks actually fail — proves severity tiers + alerting, not just green dashboards
- [ ] Parameterise volume + a `--seed` for reproducibility; document run command in harness README

---

## 3. Reference flows (each exercises a distinct DataQ run path)

### 3a. Flow A — load to warehouse  →  `SnowflakeCheckRunner` / `UnityCatalogCheckRunner`

> **Datasets:** **Snowflake** ← Orders (header+line) · Payments/Finance · Returns/Cancellations · Customer master · Product/SKU master (transactional + financial core; custom-SQL + line-grain + `sum(line_total)=order_total` + `order_total↔payment↔refund` reconciliation). **Unity Catalog (plain load)** ← Location · Promotions/pricing/coupons · Channel/Marketplace (governed reference data — proves the UC runner *without* the medallion machinery).

- [ ] File lands in ADLS landing zone (from §2 generator)
- [ ] **ADF pipeline** (or Airflow DAG) picks the file up and loads it into Snowflake / UC
- [ ] On success, the orchestrator hits DataQ's trigger path (ADF webhook / Airflow callback → trigger binding)
- [ ] DataQ suite runs against the loaded **warehouse table**; result row persists
- [ ] Verify in `/results`: run succeeded, severity derived, metric_value populated
- [ ] **Snowflake suite** exercises a **custom-SQL** check (ADR 0019) — e.g. the `sum(line_total)=order_total` / `order_total↔payment↔refund` aggregate — not just column expectations
- [ ] **UC plain-load suite** runs on a Location / Promotions table (no Silver/Gold transform — that's Flow B)

### 3b. Flow B — medallion  →  UC tables

> **Datasets:** **Feedback/survey/chatbot** (bronze raw JSON/text → silver cleaned + **PII-handled** [#226] → gold sentiment/NPS feature table — the lakehouse/ML "feature store" story) and **Sales → revenue marts** (bronze raw sales events → silver cleaned daily sales by store·channel → gold revenue/units rollups).

- [ ] File lands in **UC RAW (bronze)** layer
- [ ] **Databricks notebook** (external, not git-tracked) runs: bronze → silver → gold transforms
- [ ] DataQ suites run against the UC Silver/Gold tables
- [ ] **Gold tables host the reserved freshness/volume/anomaly targets** (ADR 0012 — daily aggregates that should refresh; "did the rollup load / did volume drop")
- [ ] **Cross-platform reconciliation target** (ADR 0014): Snowflake `Orders.order_total` ↔ Databricks `gold.daily_revenue` should tie out (the `comparison` demo; built post-v1, shaped now)
- [ ] (Optional) notebook completion triggers the DataQ run via Airflow/ADF binding
- [ ] Verify results per layer (bronze schema-drift, silver PII-redaction, gold freshness/volume)

### 3c. Flow C — flat-file in place  →  `FlatFileCheckRunner` + batch resolution

> **Datasets:** **Logistics tracking events** (ADLS · **CSV** · event-append — `tracking_number` stable across `Ready For Shipment → In Transit → Delivered` batches) and **Inventory daily snapshot** (ADLS · **Parquet** · daily-snapshot — exercises the zero-copy Arrow read, PR #140). One of each format × each batch shape (event-append vs daily-snapshot).

- [ ] File lands in ADLS and **stays a flat file** (no warehouse load)
- [ ] DataQ flat-file connection + suite targets the path with a **batching regex**
- [ ] Run resolves the **latest** batch and checks it directly
- [ ] Verify batch resolution picks the right file + result persists
- [ ] **CSV** path: tracking events — assert the tracking-number / event-status checks + freshness ("last drop < 1h")
- [ ] **Parquet** path: inventory snapshot — assert volume band (±20%) + freshness on the latest snapshot

---

## 4. QA/QE agent — module-by-module + E2E (this IS git-tracked: `.claude/agents/`)

> The one in-repo deliverable. A Claude Code subagent, sibling to `migration-safety` /
> `orchestration-abstraction-guard`. Lands in Week 8 / ongoing.

- [ ] Define agent scope: per-module functional checks (connections, suites, checks, run paths, profiler, results, orchestration) + full E2E against the harness
- [ ] Write `.claude/agents/qa-qe.md` (tools, model, system prompt)
- [ ] Wire it to drive the three flows above as E2E smoke once the harness is up
- [ ] Decide invocation cadence (per-PR? on-demand? pre-release?)
- [ ] **This file is committed** — open a PR per working agreements (branch `feature/qa-qe-agent`)

---

## 5. HashiCorp Vault — secret-management spike (post-v1 / backlog)

> Validates the `SecretStore` seam (ADR 0010/0013): Azure Key Vault is **one** impl, not the architecture.

- [ ] Stand up Vault locally (dev mode / Docker) in the harness
- [ ] Prototype a `VaultSecretStore` against the existing `SecretStore` interface (spike branch, not merged)
- [ ] Confirm no interface change needed (if it needs one, that's a finding worth an ADR note)
- [ ] Write up: does the seam hold? effort to make Vault a first-class option? → backlog issue / ADR follow-up

---

## 6. Performance / scale testing (post-v1 / backlog)

> Rides §2's parameterizable volume (`--seed` + a `--volume` knob): generate **S / M / L / XL** of the same
> datasets, run, measure, record. **Baseline-first** — establish numbers + a regression budget; no hard SLAs
> yet (set them once we know where the knees are). Standalone harness script; the **QA/QE agent (§4)** can
> invoke it later as a perf-smoke. Needs §2 (generators) + §3 (the run paths) green first.

**Lead axes (do these first):**
- [ ] **Run scaling** *(headline)* — GX run wall-clock vs **rows × #checks-per-suite**, per datasource (Snowflake · UC · flat-file CSV+Parquet). Capture throughput (rows/sec) + **worker memory peak** (GX pulls the batch in-memory) — and note whether time is in the warehouse query or GX itself.
- [ ] **Profiler latency** — profile a **wide** table (many columns) to quantify the known **N+1 per-column top-values** cost ([#327](https://github.com/TheurgicDuke771/DataQ/issues/327)) and the payoff once it's batched.
- [ ] **DB growth** — p95 latency of `/results`, `/dashboard/summary`, `/runs` as `results`/`runs` grow to 100k+ rows; validates the retention-sweep index ([#323](https://github.com/TheurgicDuke771/DataQ/issues/323)).

**Secondary axes:**
- [ ] **Concurrency / throughput** — N suites at once: Celery pool saturation, the 60s scheduler beat under many due schedules (`FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED`), run-now storms → queue wait time.
- [ ] **Flat-file batch resolution at scale** — resolving the latest batch among a large landing zone (thousands of files) — the list+regex cost.

**Method:**
- [ ] One script: param = datasource × volume tier × #checks; emits a CSV/JSON of (metric, value) per run
- [ ] Record a **baseline table** (commit it with the QA/QE agent in §4) + a **regression budget** (e.g. fail if a tier regresses > X% vs baseline) — so perf regressions are caught, not just measured once
- [ ] Capture both **app** metrics (run duration, profiler latency, API p95) and **resource** metrics (worker mem peak, DB CPU)
- [ ] Feed findings back: confirm/close #327 (profiler) + #323 (retention index); file new issues for any knee found

---

## Tracking notes

- [ ] File the milestoned GitHub issues (per convention): Terraform → W7 · mock-data + Flows A/B/C → W7 · QA/QE agent → W8 · Vault → "Backlog (post-v1 / testing)" · perf/scale testing → "Backlog (post-v1 / testing)"  *(currently NOT filed — pending decision)*
- [ ] When a flow first runs green end-to-end, update `docs/progress.md` (flip the ⬜ in the workstream table) and note the live smoke is discharged in CLAUDE.md §13

1	# DataQ harness — local secrets. NEVER commit this file.
2	# Usage: source secrets.sh  (before running terraform or snow commands)

input.content:
# ADLS Gen2 — the flat-file landing zone (Flow C in place; Flow A/B source files).
# HNS enabled = true makes this a Gen2 (hierarchical) account, not plain blob.

resource "azurerm_storage_account" "landing" {
  name                     = local.storage_account_name
  resource_group_name      = data.azurerm_resource_group.dataq.name
  location                 = var.azure_location
  account_tier             = "Standard"
  account_replication_type = "LRS"
  account_kind             = "StorageV2"
  is_hns_enabled           = true # Gen2 hierarchical namespace
  min_tls_version          = "TLS1_2"

  tags = local.common_tags
}

resource "azurerm_storage_data_lake_gen2_filesystem" "zones" {
  for_each           = toset(var.adls_filesystems)
  name               = each.value
  storage_account_id = azurerm_storage_account.landing.id
}

input.content:
# DataQ harness — Terraform (§1 of HARNESS_TODO.md)

Provisions the **datasources** for the live test/demo-data environment (ADR 0021):
ADLS Gen2 landing zone, Snowflake DB/warehouse/reader-role, Unity Catalog
catalog+schemas, and the ADF factory. Airflow is local Docker (§3), not provisioned here.

**Secret-free by design:** every provider reads its credentials from the environment
exported by `secrets.sh`. No passwords/keys/tokens live in `.tf` files or outputs.

## Usage

```bash
cd ~/Coding/Python/DataQ-harness
source secrets.sh                 # exports ARM_* / SNOWFLAKE_* / DATABRICKS_* 
cd terraform
terraform init
terraform validate
terraform plan                    # review — creates nothing
terraform apply                   # provisions real, billable resources
```

After `apply`, copy outputs back into `secrets.sh` blanks:
`SNOWFLAKE_DATABASE`, `SNOWFLAKE_WAREHOUSE`, `ADF_FACTORY_NAME` (and switch
`SNOWFLAKE_ROLE` to the reader role once Flow A has loaded tables).

```bash
terraform output                  # connection coordinates for DataQ's connection editor
```

## Teardown (the env is disposable)

```bash
terraform destroy
```

## What's where

| File | Resources |
|---|---|
| `versions.tf`       | Terraform + provider pins, local state backend |
| `providers.tf`      | Provider blocks (creds from env) |
| `variables.tf`      | Non-secret config (regions, names) |
| `main.tf`           | RG data source, random suffix, common tags |
| `adls.tf`           | ADLS Gen2 account + `landing`/`raw` filesystems |
| `snowflake.tf`      | DB + schema(s) + warehouse + least-priv reader role/grants |
| `unity_catalog.tf`  | UC catalog + raw/silver/gold/reference schemas + grants |
| `adf.tf`            | Data Factory instance (Flow A pipelines authored later) |
| `outputs.tf`        | Connection coordinates (no secrets) |

> **No Azure resource names leak into the DataQ repo** (ADR 0010/0013). They live only here.

content.text:
Two real findings from `plan` (exactly what the read-only check is for):

1. **azurerm** tries to auto-register ~60 resource providers at *subscription* scope — but the SP is Contributor on `dataq-rg` only → 403 spam. Fix: `resource_provider_registrations = "none"`.
2. **databricks** provider sees both the `ARM_*` and `DATABRICKS_*` env vars and can't decide → "more than one authorization method." Fix: pin `auth_type = "pat"`.

Both are provider-config fixes. Let me apply them.

content.content: The file /Users/arijit/Coding/Python/DataQ-harness/terraform/providers.tf has been updated successfully. (file state is current in your context — no need to Read it back)

lines: # subscription_id / tenant_id / client_id / client_secret come from ARM_* env vars.
+  # The Terraform SP is Contributor on dataq-rg ONLY — it has no rights at the
+  # subscription scope, so it cannot auto-register resource providers. Skip that;
+  # the required RPs (Microsoft.Storage, Microsoft.DataFactory) must already be
+  # registered on the subscription by an owner (see README).
# host / token come from DATABRICKS_HOST / DATABRICKS_TOKEN env vars.
+  # Pin PAT auth explicitly — the ARM_* env vars (for azurerm) would otherwise be
+  # picked up as a second, conflicting auth method (Azure SP).

content.text:
Plan is clean: **21 resources to add, 0 to change, 0 to destroy.** All three providers authenticated (Azure RG read, Snowflake reachable, Databricks current-user read). The config is apply-ready.

Let me add the one apply-time caveat (RP pre-registration) to the README and tick the tracker.

input.old_string:
terraform plan                    # review — creates nothing
terraform apply                   # provisions real, billable resources
```
input.new_string:
terraform plan                    # review — creates nothing
terraform apply                   # provisions real, billable resources
```

### Apply-time prerequisite — subscription resource providers

The Terraform SP is Contributor on `dataq-rg` only, so it **cannot register
subscription resource providers** (`resource_provider_registrations = "none"`).
Before the first `apply`, a subscription **Owner** must register the RPs the
harness uses:

```bash
az provider register --namespace Microsoft.Storage
az provider register --namespace Microsoft.DataFactory
```

`terraform plan` passes without this (it only reads the RG); `apply` of the
storage account / data factory will 403 if the RPs aren't registered.

# DataQ harness — Terraform (§1 of HARNESS_TODO.md)

Provisions the **datasources** for the live test/demo-data environment (ADR 0021):
ADLS Gen2 landing zone, Snowflake DB/warehouse/reader-role, Unity Catalog
catalog+schemas, and the ADF factory. Airflow is local Docker (§3), not provisioned here.

**Secret-free by design:** every provider reads its credentials from the environment
exported by `secrets.sh`. No passwords/keys/tokens live in `.tf` files or outputs.

## Usage

```bash
cd ~/Coding/Python/DataQ-harness
source secrets.sh                 # exports ARM_* / SNOWFLAKE_* / DATABRICKS_* 
cd terraform
terraform init
terraform validate
terraform plan                    # review — creates nothing
terraform apply                   # provisions real, billable resources
```

After `apply`, copy outputs back into `secrets.sh` blanks:
`SNOWFLAKE_DATABASE`, `SNOWFLAKE_WAREHOUSE`, `ADF_FACTORY_NAME` (and switch
`SNOWFLAKE_ROLE` to the reader role once Flow A has loaded tables).

```bash
terraform output                  # connection coordinates for DataQ's connection editor
```

## Teardown (the env is disposable)

```bash
terraform destroy
```

## What's where

| File | Resources |
|---|---|
| `versions.tf`       | Terraform + provider pins, local state backend |
| `providers.tf`      | Provider blocks (creds from env) |
| `variables.tf`      | Non-secret config (regions, names) |
| `main.tf`           | RG data source, random suffix, common tags |
| `adls.tf`           | ADLS Gen2 account + `landing`/`raw` filesystems |
| `snowflake.tf`      | DB + schema(s) + warehouse + least-priv reader role/grants |
| `unity_catalog.tf`  | UC catalog + raw/silver/gold/reference schemas + grants |
| `adf.tf`            | Data Factory instance (Flow A pipelines authored later) |
| `outputs.tf`        | Connection coordinates (no secrets) |

> **No Azure resource names leak into the DataQ repo** (ADR 0010/0013). They live only here.

lines: terraform apply                   # provisions real, billable resources
+The Terraform SP is Contributor on `dataq-rg` only, so it **cannot register
+Before the first `apply`, a subscription **Owner** must register the RPs the
+`terraform plan` passes without this (it only reads the RG); `apply` of the
+storage account / data factory will 403 if the RPs aren't registered.
After `apply`, copy outputs back into `secrets.sh` blanks:
`SNOWFLAKE_ROLE` to the reader role once Flow A has loaded tables).

content.text:
§1 Terraform scaffold is done and verified to a clean plan. I've stopped before `apply` since that provisions real billable cloud resources — that's your call to greenlight.

## What I did — §1 Terraform skeleton (in `~/Coding/Python/DataQ-harness/terraform/`)

Authored a secret-free, apply-ready config (all creds read from `secrets.sh` env vars; nothing sensitive in `.tf` files or outputs):

| File | What |
|---|---|
| `versions.tf` | Provider pins + local (gitignored) state backend |
| `providers.tf` | azurerm / snowflake / databricks / random — creds from env |
| `variables.tf` | Non-secret config (regions, names) matching §0 |
| `main.tf` | RG data source, random suffix, common `project=dataq` tags |
| `adls.tf` | ADLS Gen2 account (HNS) + `landing`/`raw` filesystems |
| `snowflake.tf` | `DATAQ_DB` + `RETAIL` schema + `DATAQ_WH` + least-priv `DATAQ_READER` role (granted to your existing user — no new password) |
| `unity_catalog.tf` | `dataq_retail` catalog + raw/silver/gold/reference schemas + grants |
| `adf.tf` | `dataq-harness-adf` factory |
| `outputs.tf` | Connection coordinates for DataQ's connection editor |

Plus a `terraform/README.md` and a harness-root `.gitignore` (state holds secrets in plaintext).

**Verification:** `init` ✓ · `validate` ✓ · `fmt` ✓ · `plan` ✓ → **21 to add, 0 change, 0 destroy**, all three providers authenticate. The plan surfaced and I fixed two real provider-config issues: azurerm tried to register ~60 RPs at subscription scope (SP only has `dataq-rg`) → `resource_provider_registrations = "none"`; databricks saw both `ARM_*` and `DATABRICKS_*` and couldn't pick → pinned `auth_type = "pat"`. Tracker updated.

**Before you can `apply`:** a subscription **Owner** must pre-register `Microsoft.Storage` and `Microsoft.DataFactory` (the SP can't self-register; documented in the README). Then `source secrets.sh && terraform apply`, and copy outputs into the `secrets.sh` blanks (`SNOWFLAKE_DATABASE/WAREHOUSE`, `ADF_FACTORY_NAME`).

Want me to proceed to `apply` (I'd confirm the RPs are registered first), or move on to §2 (mock-data generators)?

## On your side question — the Entra user `dataq-admin@royarijit04outlook.onmicrosoft.com`

**Not needed for any of the Terraform/infra harness** — all provisioning runs as the service principal `dataq-terraform-sp` (`ARM_CLIENT_ID f6ab3a4d…`), not a user. So §1 doesn't touch it.

**But keep it — it's needed later for the app-level E2E.** DataQ's auth is Azure AD (MSAL) with real user login, and the workspace-admin surface is gated by the `WORKSPACE_ADMIN_EMAILS` allowlist. When you run Flows A/B/C and the QA/QE agent (§3/§4) against the actual DataQ app, you need a human identity to log in as and to be the workspace admin — `dataq-admin@…onmicrosoft.com` is the natural fit. Add its email to `WORKSPACE_ADMIN_EMAILS`.

One thing to confirm: it's only useful if it lives in the **same tenant** DataQ's MSAL app registration trusts. Your harness SP is in tenant `07b592a3-…`; the `…onmicrosoft.com` user may be in a different default tenant. If they don't match, either move the app registration or create the admin user in the SP's tenant. Want me to check which tenant the DataQ app registration targets?

content.text:
**Confirmed — same tenant.** `royarijit04outlook.onmicrosoft.com` resolves to tenant `07b592a3-12ee-4631-abb1-8a7b73d91241`, exactly the tenant your harness SP lives in. So the `…onmicrosoft.com` suffix is just that tenant's default domain — not a separate directory.

So the Entra user is in the right place. To wire it up when you exercise the app E2E:
- Set `AZURE_TENANT_ID` / `VITE_AZURE_TENANT_ID` = `07b592a3-12ee-4631-abb1-8a7b73d91241` (the app is single-tenant; it'll then trust this user).
- Add `dataq-admin@royarijit04outlook.onmicrosoft.com` to `WORKSPACE_ADMIN_EMAILS` to make it the workspace admin.
- You'll still need to create the app registrations (`AZURE_API_CLIENT_ID` for the API, `AZURE_SPA_CLIENT_ID` for the SPA) in that tenant — those are separate from the Terraform SP, and that's app-setup (Week 7 / E2E), not part of §1.

**Net: keep the user — it's valid and correctly placed.** It's not used by the Terraform harness, but it's your app login + workspace-admin identity for the E2E flows.

content.text:
`pandas` + `pyarrow` are already in the `dataq` env; Faker is not. I'll keep the generator **dependency-light** — stdlib `random` (seeded) + curated value pools, no Faker — so it adds no new deps and gives tighter control over referential consistency (which is the hard requirement in §2). I'll run it via the `dataq` env.

Let me build the `mockdata` package. Starting with config (volume tiers + the route map from §2):

input.content:
"""Generation config: volume tiers + the §2 route map (dataset -> run path / format).

No secrets, no cloud calls — this module only describes *what* to generate and
*where each dataset lands*. The emit layer (emit.py) writes to a local out/ dir
mirroring the route map; uploading to ADLS / loading to Snowflake/UC is a later
step (§3, once Terraform §1 is applied).
"""

from __future__ import annotations

from dataclasses import dataclass, field

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Volume tiers — scale knobs for perf testing (§6 rides these via --tier).
# Counts are "base entities"; transactional tables fan out from them.
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------

VOLUME_TIERS: dict[str, dict[str, int]] = {
    "S": {"customers": 200, "products": 150, "stores": 8, "promos": 15, "orders": 800},
    "M": {"customers": 1_000, "products": 600, "stores": 25, "promos": 40, "orders": 4_000},
    "L": {"customers": 5_000, "products": 2_500, "stores": 80, "promos": 120, "orders": 20_000},
    "XL": {"customers": 20_000, "products": 8_000, "stores": 250, "promos": 300, "orders": 100_000},
}

DEFAULT_TIER = "S"


# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Route map (§2) — which dataset lands on which DataQ run path + file format.
# emit.py uses `subdir` + `fmt` to lay out the output tree.
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------


@dataclass(frozen=True)
class Route:
    """Where a generated dataset belongs in the DataQ run-path topology."""

    flow: str  # "A-snowflake" | "A-uc" | "B-medallion" | "C-flatfile"
    subdir: str  # path under out/ root
    fmt: str  # "csv" | "parquet" | "json"


ROUTES: dict[str, Route] = {
    # Flow A — load to warehouse (Snowflake): transactional + financial core.
    "products": Route("A-snowflake", "snowflake/products", "csv"),
    "customers": Route("A-snowflake", "snowflake/customers", "csv"),
    "orders_header": Route("A-snowflake", "snowflake/orders_header", "csv"),
    "order_lines": Route("A-snowflake", "snowflake/order_lines", "csv"),
    "payments": Route("A-snowflake", "snowflake/payments", "csv"),
    "refunds": Route("A-snowflake", "snowflake/refunds", "csv"),
    "returns": Route("A-snowflake", "snowflake/returns", "csv"),
    # Flow A — Unity Catalog plain load: governed reference data (no medallion).
    "locations": Route("A-uc", "unity_catalog/locations", "parquet"),
    "channels": Route("A-uc", "unity_catalog/channels", "parquet"),
    "promotions": Route("A-uc", "unity_catalog/promotions", "parquet"),
    # Flow B — medallion bronze source (Databricks notebook refines bronze->silver->gold).
    "sales_events": Route("B-medallion", "databricks_medallion/bronze/sales_events", "json"),
    "feedback": Route("B-medallion", "databricks_medallion/bronze/feedback", "json"),
    # Flow C — flat-file in place (ADLS). Batch-resolved.
    "tracking_events": Route("C-flatfile", "adls_flatfile/logistics_tracking", "csv"),
    "inventory_snapshot": Route("C-flatfile", "adls_flatfile/inventory_snapshot", "parquet"),
}


@dataclass
class GenConfig:
    """Resolved generation parameters for one run."""

    tier: str = DEFAULT_TIER
    seed: int = 42
    out_dir: str = "out"
    inject_issues: bool = True
    issue_rate: float = 0.03  # fraction of rows touched by deliberate DQ issues
    snapshots: int = 5  # inventory daily snapshots (exercises batch resolution + freshness)
    tracking_batches: tuple[str, ...] = ("ready", "in_transit", "delivered")
    counts: dict[str, int] = field(default_factory=dict)

    def resolve(self) -> "GenConfig":
        if self.tier not in VOLUME_TIERS:
            raise ValueError(f"unknown tier {self.tier!r}; choose from {list(VOLUME_TIERS)}")
        self.counts = dict(VOLUME_TIERS[self.tier])
        return self

"""Curated retail value pools. Believable, low-cardinality dimensions so checks /
profiling / severity look real. PII pools (names, email/phone shapes) are the
redaction targets for sample-row handling (#226)."""

from __future__ import annotations

FIRST_NAMES = [
    "Aiden", "Maya", "Liam", "Sofia", "Noah", "Priya", "Ethan", "Zara",
    "Lucas", "Amara", "Mason", "Nina", "Kai", "Leah", "Omar", "Iris",
    "Ravi", "Elena", "Theo", "Yuki",
]
LAST_NAMES = [
    "Patel", "Nguyen", "Garcia", "Khan", "Silva", "Chen", "Okafor", "Rossi",
    "Kim", "Haddad", "Novak", "Tran", "Diaz", "Ahmed", "Mori", "Costa",
]
STREETS = ["Maple Ave", "Oak St", "Cedar Ln", "Birch Rd", "Elm Way", "Pine Blvd"]
CITIES = [
    ("Seattle", "WA"), ("Portland", "OR"), ("Austin", "TX"), ("Denver", "CO"),
    ("Chicago", "IL"), ("Boston", "MA"), ("Atlanta", "GA"), ("Phoenix", "AZ"),
]

PRODUCT_CATEGORIES = ["Apparel", "Footwear", "Accessories", "Home", "Beauty"]
COLORS = ["Black", "White", "Navy", "Crimson", "Olive", "Sand", "Slate", "Teal"]
MATERIALS = ["Cotton", "Wool", "Leather", "Linen", "Polyester", "Denim", "Silk"]

CHANNELS = ["online", "store", "marketplace"]  # first-class dimension (§2)
CHANNEL_WEIGHTS = [0.5, 0.35, 0.15]
