toolUseResult.originalFile:
"""CLI for the DataQ harness mock-data generator.

Three subcommands:
  backfill   one-shot full generation (§2) — masters + N snapshots + deliberate issues
  init       create persisted state + emit the master/reference loads once (§2a)
  tick       emit ONE incremental batch for a feed (§2a) — driven on a cadence by
             Airflow/ADF schedules (orders 4x/day, tracking 2x/day, inventory daily, ...)

Reproducible per (--tier, --seed) except wall-clock-anchored timestamps (so the
latest snapshots/batches are genuinely fresh — see generate.py).
"""

from __future__ import annotations

import argparse

from .config import DEFAULT_TIER, VOLUME_TIERS, GenConfig
from .dq_issues import inject
from .emit import write, write_batch
from .generate import generate
from .increment import FEEDS, init_state, tick
from .state import load_masters, load_state, save_state


def _common(parser: argparse.ArgumentParser) -> None:
    parser.add_argument("--tier", choices=list(VOLUME_TIERS), default=DEFAULT_TIER)
    parser.add_argument("--seed", type=int, default=42)
    parser.add_argument("--out", default="out", help="output directory root")
    parser.add_argument("--state", default="state", help="state directory (§2a)")


def main(argv: list[str] | None = None) -> int:
    parser = argparse.ArgumentParser(prog="mockdata", description=__doc__)
    sub = parser.add_subparsers(dest="cmd", required=True)

    p_back = sub.add_parser("backfill", help="one-shot full generation (§2)")
    _common(p_back)
    p_back.add_argument("--snapshots", type=int, default=5)
    p_back.add_argument("--issue-rate", type=float, default=0.03)
    p_back.add_argument(
        "--no-issues",
        dest="inject_issues",
        action="store_false",
        help="emit clean data (no deliberate DQ issues)",
    )

    p_init = sub.add_parser("init", help="create state + emit reference loads (§2a)")
    _common(p_init)

    p_tick = sub.add_parser("tick", help="emit one incremental batch for a feed (§2a)")
    _common(p_tick)
    p_tick.add_argument("--feed", required=True, choices=list(FEEDS))
    p_tick.add_argument(
        "--count", type=int, default=None, help="batch size (orders/feedback)"
    )
    p_tick.add_argument(
        "--issues", action="store_true", help="inject row-level DQ issues"
    )
    p_tick.add_argument(
        "--volume-drop", action="store_true", help="(inventory) thin snapshot ~30%"
    )
    p_tick.add_argument(
        "--stale", action="store_true", help="(inventory) push load_ts +26h back"
    )
    p_tick.add_argument(
        "--schema-drift", action="store_true", help="(inventory) rename on_hand"
    )

    args = parser.parse_args(argv)
    config = GenConfig(
        tier=args.tier, seed=args.seed, out_dir=args.out, state_dir=args.state
    ).resolve()

    if args.cmd == "backfill":
        return _run_backfill(config, args)
    if args.cmd == "init":
        return _run_init(config)
    return _run_tick(config, args)


def _run_backfill(config: GenConfig, args: argparse.Namespace) -> int:
    config.inject_issues = args.inject_issues
    config.issue_rate = args.issue_rate
    config.snapshots = args.snapshots
    print(f"backfill tier={config.tier} seed={config.seed} -> {config.out_dir}/")
    datasets = generate(config)
    issues = inject(datasets, config) if config.inject_issues else []
    manifest = write(datasets, config, issues)
    total = sum(m["rows"] for m in manifest.values())
    print(f"  {len(manifest)} datasets, {total:,} rows")
    for name, m in manifest.items():
        print(
            f"    {name:<20} {m['rows']:>8,} rows  [{m['flow']}]  {len(m['files'])} file(s)"
        )
    if issues:
        print(
            f"  {len(issues)} deliberate DQ issues -> {config.out_dir}/_dq_issues_manifest.json"
        )
    return 0


def _run_init(config: GenConfig) -> int:
    print(f"init state={config.state_dir}/ tier={config.tier} seed={config.seed}")
    masters, _state = init_state(config)
    # Emit the reference/master loads once (Flow A Snowflake + UC reference data).
    manifest = write(masters, config, [])
    for name, m in manifest.items():
        print(f"    {name:<20} {m['rows']:>6,} rows  [{m['flow']}]")
    print(f"  state initialised -> {config.state_dir}/state.json (+ masters/)")
    print("  now drive ticks on a cadence, e.g.:")
    print("    python -m mockdata tick --feed orders     # 4x/day")
    print("    python -m mockdata tick --feed inventory  # daily")
    return 0


def _run_tick(config: GenConfig, args: argparse.Namespace) -> int:
    state = load_state(config.state_dir)
    masters = load_masters(config.state_dir)
    datasets, issues = tick(
        config,
        state,
        masters,
        args.feed,
        args.count,
        inject_issues=args.issues,
        volume_drop=args.volume_drop,
        stale=args.stale,
        schema_drift=args.schema_drift,
    )
    batch_id = f"b{state.batch_counter:06d}"
    entry = write_batch(datasets, config, batch_id, args.feed, issues)
    save_state(config.state_dir, state)

    rows = sum(d["rows"] for d in entry["datasets"].values())
    print(
        f"tick #{state.batch_counter} feed={args.feed} batch={batch_id}: {rows:,} rows"
    )
    for name, d in entry["datasets"].items():
        print(f"    {name:<20} {d['rows']:>6,} rows -> {d['file']}")
    if args.feed in ("orders", "tracking"):
        print(f"    open shipments carried forward: {len(state.open_shipments)}")
    if issues:
        for it in issues:
            print(
                f"    issue: {it['kind']:<14} {it['dataset']:<20} n={it['n']} {it['detail']}"
            )
    return 0


if __name__ == "__main__":
    raise SystemExit(main())

  backfill   one-shot full generation (§2) — masters + N snapshots + deliberate issues
  init       create persisted state + emit the master/reference loads once (§2a)
  tick       emit ONE incremental batch for a feed (§2a) — driven on a cadence by
             Airflow/ADF schedules (orders 4x/day, tracking 2x/day, inventory daily, ...)

Reproducible per (--tier, --seed) except wall-clock-anchored timestamps (so the
latest snapshots/batches are genuinely fresh — see generate.py).
"""

from __future__ import annotations

import argparse

from .config import DEFAULT_TIER, VOLUME_TIERS, GenConfig
from .dq_issues import inject
from .emit import write, write_batch
from .generate import generate
from .increment import FEEDS, init_state, tick
from .state import load_masters, load_state, make_backend, save_state


def _common(parser: argparse.ArgumentParser) -> None:
    parser.add_argument("--tier", choices=list(VOLUME_TIERS), default=DEFAULT_TIER)
    parser.add_argument("--seed", type=int, default=42)
    parser.add_argument("--out", default="out", help="output directory root")
    parser.add_argument("--state", default="state", help="state directory (§2a)")


def main(argv: list[str] | None = None) -> int:
    parser = argparse.ArgumentParser(prog="mockdata", description=__doc__)
    sub = parser.add_subparsers(dest="cmd", required=True)

    p_back = sub.add_parser("backfill", help="one-shot full generation (§2)")
    _common(p_back)
    p_back.add_argument("--snapshots", type=int, default=5)

        "--no-issues",

        action="store_false",
        help="emit clean data (no deliberate DQ issues)",
    )

input.content:
# mockdata image — runs as the Container Apps Job (cron) that produces files into
# the ADLS landing zone (§2a "file creation" layer). Same Python runtime as DataQ (3.13).
#
# Build (from harness root):  docker build -t mockdata:latest .
# Local smoke:                docker run --rm -v "$PWD/out:/data/out" -v "$PWD/state:/data/state" \
#                               mockdata:latest init --out /data/out --state /data/state
# Job invocation (per feed):  ... tick --feed inventory --out adls://<acct>/landing --state adls://<acct>/landing/state

FROM python:3.13-slim

WORKDIR /app

# Deps first for layer caching. pyarrow/pandas wheels are prebuilt for slim.
COPY mockdata/requirements.txt mockdata/requirements.txt
RUN pip install --no-cache-dir -r mockdata/requirements.txt

# The package itself.
COPY mockdata/ mockdata/

# `python -m mockdata <subcommand>` — args supplied by the Job's command/args.
ENTRYPOINT ["python", "-m", "mockdata"]
CMD ["--help"]

# Local smoke:                docker run --rm -v "$PWD/out:/data/out" -v "$PWD/state:/data/state" \
#                               mockdata:latest init --out /data/out --state /data/state
# Job invocation (per feed):  ... tick --feed inventory --out adls://<acct>/landing --state adls://<acct>/landing/state

FROM python:3.13-slim

WORKDIR /app

# Deps first for layer caching. pyarrow/pandas wheels are prebuilt for slim.
COPY mockdata/requirements.txt mockdata/requirements.txt
RUN pip install --no-cache-dir -r mockdata/requirements.txt

# The package itself.
COPY mockdata/ mockdata/

# `python -m mockdata <subcommand>` — args supplied by the Job's command/args.
ENTRYPOINT ["python", "-m", "mockdata"]
CMD ["--help"]

119	>       (e.g. Job drops inventory 01:00 → scheduled load 01:15) so there's data to pick up.
120	>     - _Why both:_ exercises both ADF/Airflow trigger types **and** DataQ's pipeline-run monitoring +
121	>       10-min polling / gap-recovery fallback (the poll only meaningfully bites on scheduled pipelines).
122	>   Flow C (flat-file in place) has no load step — the Job's file drop *is* the arrival; DataQ reads

125	>   **feedback → schedule** — one event + one schedule per warehouse target (Snowflake & UC) for parity.

127	>   state must move off local disk to **blob** (ADLS). `state.py` needs a pluggable backend

(`mockdata/increment.py` + `state.py`)
- [x] **Persisted state** (`state/state.json` + `state/masters/*.parquet`): master pools + climbing id counters (`order_number` never collides) + open-shipment registry (a `tracking_number` from an orders tick gets `ready→in_transit→delivered` on successive tracking ticks). **Verified:** 2 order ticks → ranges 1–40 / 41–80, 0 collisions; tracking# identical across the 3 lifecycle batches; cross-feed FK integrity holds; ruff+black clean
- [x] **Per-feed cadence map** (cron) — documented in `mockdata/README.md`: orders `0 0,6,12,18 * * *` · tracking `0 0,12 * * *` · inventory `0 1 * * *` · feedback/reference `0 1 */2 * *`; masters via `init` (rare). Anomaly flags `--volume-drop`/`--stale`/`--schema-drift` + `--issues` per tick
- [ ] **`state.py` pluggable backend** (buildable now, no cloud): abstract load/save behind a `StateBackend` seam — `LocalStateBackend` (today) + `AdlsStateBackend` (blob, for the ephemeral Job). State (state.json + masters/) lives in an ADLS `state/` prefix so ticks survive Job restarts
- [ ] **`Dockerfile`** for the `mockdata` image (buildable+testable now via local `docker run`): pandas/pyarrow + the package; entrypoint `python -m mockdata`
- [ ] **Container Apps Job (cron)** per feed — Terraform (`azurerm_container_app_job` with `schedule_trigger`) running the image with the per-feed cron + `tick --feed <name>`; needs a Container Apps Environment + registry for the image (apply-gated, §1/§3)
- [ ] **ADF/Airflow load+trigger** (§3, post-apply) — **mix event + schedule triggers** (see decision above): event/file-landing for orders+tracking, schedule/time for inventory+feedback; `load` (Snowflake/UC) → `on_success` → DataQ trigger binding. Schedule loads offset *after* the file-creation Job. Flow C = no load (DataQ reads in place)

input.old_string:
So referential consistency holds **across ticks**, not just within one run — the same
guarantee the backfill gives, extended over time. Cadence itself is driven externally
by **Airflow/ADF schedules** (§2a decision): one scheduled DAG per feed runs
`tick → load/upload → on-success triggers DataQ`. cron mapping: orders `0 0,6,12,18 * * *`
· tracking `0 0,12 * * *` · inventory `0 1 * * *` · feedback `0 1 */2 * *`.
input.new_string:
So referential consistency holds **across ticks**, not just within one run — the same
guarantee the backfill gives, extended over time.

**State backend.** `--state` accepts a local path *or* `adls://<account>/<filesystem>/<prefix>`
(`state.py` `StateBackend` seam: `LocalStateBackend` / `AdlsStateBackend`). The cron
**Container Apps Job** is ephemeral, so it points `--state` at ADLS blob (auth via
`DefaultAzureCredential` — managed identity in the Job, `az login` locally); local runs
default to the filesystem. Build/run the image via the repo-root `Dockerfile`.

**Cadence engine = Container Apps Job (cron)** for file creation; **Airflow/ADF** then
load + trigger DataQ (a mix of file-landing-event and scheduled-time triggers). cron
mapping: orders `0 0,6,12,18 * * *` · tracking `0 0,12 * * *` · inventory `0 1 * * *`
· feedback `0 1 */2 * *`.

toolUseResult.originalFile:
# DataQ harness — mock-data generator (§2 of HARNESS_TODO.md)

Believable, **referentially-consistent** retail data across the four DataQ run
paths, with **deliberate DQ issues seeded and manifested** so checks / severity /
alerting have something real to bite on (not just green dashboards).

Dependency-light: **pandas + pyarrow + stdlib only** (no Faker — stdlib `random`
+ curated pools give tighter control over the shared-key consistency §2 needs).

## Run

```bash
conda activate dataq            # has pandas + pyarrow
cd ~/Coding/Python/DataQ-harness
```

Three subcommands:

```bash
# §2  one-shot full backfill (masters + N snapshots + deliberate issues)
python -m mockdata backfill --tier S --seed 42 --out ./out

# §2a recurring cadence — init once, then tick each feed on a schedule
python -m mockdata init --out ./out --state ./state
python -m mockdata tick --feed orders     --out ./out --state ./state   # 4x/day
python -m mockdata tick --feed tracking   --out ./out --state ./state   # 2x/day
python -m mockdata tick --feed inventory  --out ./out --state ./state   # daily
python -m mockdata tick --feed feedback   --out ./out --state ./state   # every 2 days
```

Common flags: `--tier S/M/L/XL` (perf §6), `--seed`, `--out`, `--state`.
`backfill` adds `--snapshots N`, `--issue-rate`, `--no-issues`.
`tick` adds `--feed {orders,tracking,inventory,feedback}`, `--count N`
(orders/feedback batch size), `--issues` (row-level), and inventory anomaly flags
`--volume-drop` / `--stale` / `--schema-drift`.

> **Timestamps are anchored to generation wall-clock `now`** (offsets are seeded):
> the *latest* inventory snapshot and the tracking `delivered` batch are genuinely
> fresh, so the reserved **freshness** monitors (ADR 0012) bite. Everything else is
> reproducible per `--seed`.

## What it emits (the §2 route map)

| Dataset(s) | Flow / run path | Format |
|---|---|---|
| products, customers, orders_header, order_lines, payments, refunds, returns | **A — Snowflake** (custom-SQL, line-grain, reconciliation) | CSV |
| locations, channels, promotions | **A — Unity Catalog** plain load (reference data) | Parquet |
| sales_events, feedback | **B — Databricks medallion** bronze source | NDJSON |
| tracking_events (event-append) | **C — flat-file** in place (ADLS) | CSV per batch |
| inventory_snapshot (daily) | **C — flat-file** in place (ADLS) | Parquet per date |

Output tree mirrors the route map (`out/snowflake/…`, `out/unity_catalog/…`,
`out/databricks_medallion/bronze/…`, `out/adls_flatfile/…`). Two sidecar manifests
land at the `out/` root:

- `_manifest.json` — dataset → `{flow, format, files, rows}`
- `_dq_issues_manifest.json` — the deliberate failures, each with the affected `keys`

## Referential consistency (the hard part)

One system-of-record per entity; shared key pools mean the same id denotes the same
entity everywhere:

- `order_number` ties Orders ↔ Payments ↔ Refunds ↔ Returns ↔ Sales events ↔ Tracking
- `sku_id` ties Products ↔ Order lines ↔ Inventory ↔ Returns
- one `tracking_number` is stable across `ready → in_transit → delivered` batch files

So the cross-dataset reconciliation / FK-orphan / join checks measure **real** DQ —
the only inconsistencies are the deliberate ones.

## Reserved-monitor-kind shaping (ADR 0012/0014 — built post-v1, shaped now)

- **Freshness / volume / anomaly** → `inventory_snapshot` (timestamped `load_ts`,
  daily batches; one snapshot is thinned for a volume-band/anomaly hit)
- **Schema drift** → newest `inventory_snapshot` renames `on_hand → qty_on_hand`
- **Comparison / reconciliation** → in-dataset `sum(line_total)+tax == order_total`;
  cross-table `order_total ↔ payment.captured ↔ refund`; cross-platform
  Snowflake `order_total` ↔ Databricks `gold.daily_revenue` (sales_events roll-up)

## Deliberate DQ issues (default on)

nulls (channel, email) · duplicates (order_lines, product sku) · FK orphans
(`SKU-000000`, `CUST-000000`) · reconciliation mismatch (order_total, payment) ·
stale freshness (latest snapshot `load_ts` +26h back) · volume drop (mid snapshot
thinned to ~30%) · schema drift (newest snapshot). All recorded in
`_dq_issues_manifest.json` with affected keys.

## Recurring cadence (§2a)

`init` generates + persists the **master pools** (`state/masters/*.parquet`) and emits
the reference/warehouse loads once. Each `tick` then emits **one** batch-suffixed file
per dataset (`<name>_b000123.<fmt>` — ADLS landing zones are append-style) and advances
`state/state.json`:

- climbing id counters → `order_number` never collides across ticks
- an **open-shipment registry** → a `tracking_number` created on an orders tick gets its
  `ready_for_shipment → in_transit → delivered` events on *successive* tracking ticks
- `_ticks.jsonl` logs every batch; `_dq_issues_manifest.jsonl` appends per-tick issues

So referential consistency holds **across ticks**, not just within one run — the same
guarantee the backfill gives, extended over time. Cadence itself is driven externally
by **Airflow/ADF schedules** (§2a decision): one scheduled DAG per feed runs
`tick → load/upload → on-success triggers DataQ`. cron mapping: orders `0 0,6,12,18 * * *`
· tracking `0 0,12 * * *` · inventory `0 1 * * *` · feedback `0 1 */2 * *`.

## Next (§3)

`out/` is local. Uploading to the ADLS landing zone + COPY INTO Snowflake / load to
UC (and wiring the Airflow DAGs / ADF triggers that call these ticks) happens in §3
once Terraform (§1) is applied. The folder layout is already the landing-zone shape.

Both built and verified.

## §2a state backend + Dockerfile — done

**`state.py` pluggable backend**
- `StateBackend` seam → `LocalStateBackend` (default) + `AdlsStateBackend` (Gen2 blob via `DefaultAzureCredential`; azure libs lazy-imported so local runs need neither).
- `make_backend()` picks by URI: `adls://<acct>/<fs>/<prefix>` → ADLS, anything else → local path.
- Verified the local tick flow is intact after the refactor (counters climb, ruff+black clean). The ADLS impl is coded; runnable against real ADLS only post-apply.

**Docker image** (`Dockerfile` + `.dockerignore` at harness root)
- `python:3.13-slim` + pandas/pyarrow/azure, `ENTRYPOINT python -m mockdata`; `.dockerignore` keeps `secrets.sh`/terraform/out/state out of the build context.
- Verified end-to-end: image builds, in-container `init`+`tick` write the route layout, **state survives across separate container runs**, `azure SDK OK` in the image.

## One honest gap this surfaced (flagged in the tracker, not silently dropped)

I built the **state** backend, but the **output** writer (`emit.py`'s `--out`) still targets local FS / a mount. In my container smoke test that worked because I mounted a volume — but the real ephemeral Job has **no mount**, so for it to drop files into the landing zone, `--out adls://…` needs the *same* ADLS routing the state backend now has. That's the **remaining piece before the Job is fully standalone** — a sibling change to `emit.py`, buildable now (no cloud needed to write the code; only live ADLS to fully test).

It's small and symmetric with what I just did. Want me to do the **emit→ADLS output backend** now so the image is a complete, self-contained Job — or pause here and pick it up when we tackle the Container Apps Job Terraform (which is apply-gated anyway)?

"""Write generated datasets to a local out/ tree mirroring the §2 route map.

Layout (out/ root):
  snowflake/<table>/<table>.csv                 Flow A -> Snowflake (COPY INTO source)
  unity_catalog/<table>/<table>.parquet         Flow A -> UC plain load
  databricks_medallion/bronze/<src>/<src>.json  Flow B -> medallion bronze (NDJSON)
  adls_flatfile/logistics_tracking/tracking_events_<batch>.csv   Flow C (event-append)
  adls_flatfile/inventory_snapshot/inventory_<date>.parquet      Flow C (daily snapshot)

Two split datasets become multiple files:
  tracking_events  -> one CSV per `batch`        (same tracking_number recurs across files)
  inventory_snapshot -> one Parquet per `snapshot_date`

Schema-drift (the one cross-file deliberate issue) is applied here: the newest
inventory snapshot file renames on_hand -> qty_on_hand, appended to the DQ manifest.

Writes two sidecar manifests at the out/ root:
  _manifest.json            dataset -> {flow, format, files, rows}
  _dq_issues_manifest.json  the deliberate failures (row-level from dq_issues + schema drift)
"""

from __future__ import annotations

import json
from pathlib import Path

import pandas as pd

from .config import ROUTES, GenConfig


def write(
    datasets: dict[str, pd.DataFrame], config: GenConfig, dq_issues: list[dict]
) -> dict:
    root = Path(config.out_dir)
    root.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)
    file_manifest: dict[str, dict] = {}

    for name, df in datasets.items():
        route = ROUTES[name]
        target = root / route.subdir
        target.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)

        if name == "tracking_events":
            files = _write_tracking(df, target)
        elif name == "inventory_snapshot":
            files, drift = _write_inventory(df, target, config.inject_issues)
            if drift:
                dq_issues.append(drift)
        else:
            files = [_write_single(df, target, name, route.fmt)]

        file_manifest[name] = {
            "flow": route.flow,
            "format": route.fmt,
            "rows": len(df),
            "files": [str(p.relative_to(root)) for p in files],
        }

    (root / "_manifest.json").write_text(
        json.dumps(file_manifest, indent=2, default=str)
    )
    (root / "_dq_issues_manifest.json").write_text(
        json.dumps(dq_issues, indent=2, default=str)
    )
    return file_manifest


def _write_single(df: pd.DataFrame, target: Path, name: str, fmt: str) -> Path:
    path = target / f"{name}.{fmt}"
    _write_df(df, path, fmt)
    return path


def _write_df(df: pd.DataFrame, path: Path, fmt: str) -> None:
    if fmt == "csv":
        df.to_csv(path, index=False)
    elif fmt == "parquet":
        df.to_parquet(path, index=False)
    elif fmt == "json":  # NDJSON bronze raw
        df.to_json(path, orient="records", lines=True, date_format="iso")
    else:
        raise ValueError(f"unknown format {fmt!r}")


def write_batch(
    datasets: dict[str, pd.DataFrame],
    config: GenConfig,
    batch_id: str,
    feed: str,
    issues: list[dict],
) -> dict:
    """Write one incremental tick (§2a): batch-suffixed files into the route subdirs.

    ADLS landing zones are append-style (a new file per load), so each tick lands a
    distinct `<name>_<batch_id>.<fmt>` rather than overwriting. Appends one line to
    out/_ticks.jsonl and (per issue) out/_dq_issues_manifest.jsonl.
    """
    root = Path(config.out_dir)
    entry: dict = {"batch_id": batch_id, "feed": feed, "datasets": {}}
    for name, df in datasets.items():
        route = ROUTES[name]
        target = root / route.subdir
        target.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)
        path = target / f"{name}_{batch_id}.{route.fmt}"
        _write_df(df, path, route.fmt)
        entry["datasets"][name] = {
            "rows": len(df),
            "file": str(path.relative_to(root)),
        }

    with (root / "_ticks.jsonl").open("a") as f:
        f.write(json.dumps(entry, default=str) + "\n")
    if issues:
        with (root / "_dq_issues_manifest.jsonl").open("a") as f:
            for it in issues:
                f.write(json.dumps({**it, "batch_id": batch_id}, default=str) + "\n")
    return entry


def _write_tracking(df: pd.DataFrame, target: Path) -> list[Path]:
    paths = []
    for batch, group in df.groupby("batch"):
        path = target / f"tracking_events_{batch}.csv"
        group.drop(columns=["batch"]).to_csv(path, index=False)
        paths.append(path)
    return paths


def _write_inventory(
    df: pd.DataFrame, target: Path, inject_drift: bool
) -> tuple[list[Path], dict | None]:
    paths = []
    drift: dict | None = None
    latest_date = df["snapshot_date"].max()
    for snap_date, group in df.groupby("snapshot_date"):
        out = group.drop(columns=["snapshot_date"])
        if inject_drift and snap_date == latest_date:
            out = out.rename(
                columns={"on_hand": "qty_on_hand"}
            )  # schema drift on newest load
            drift = {
                "kind": "schema_drift",
                "dataset": "inventory_snapshot",
                "detail": f"newest snapshot {snap_date} renames on_hand -> qty_on_hand",
                "n": 1,
                "keys": [str(snap_date)],
            }
        path = target / f"inventory_{snap_date}.parquet"
        out.to_parquet(path, index=False)
        paths.append(path)
    return paths, drift

toolUseResult.originalFile:
"""CLI for the DataQ harness mock-data generator.

Three subcommands:
  backfill   one-shot full generation (§2) — masters + N snapshots + deliberate issues
  init       create persisted state + emit the master/reference loads once (§2a)
  tick       emit ONE incremental batch for a feed (§2a) — driven on a cadence by
             Airflow/ADF schedules (orders 4x/day, tracking 2x/day, inventory daily, ...)

Reproducible per (--tier, --seed) except wall-clock-anchored timestamps (so the
latest snapshots/batches are genuinely fresh — see generate.py).
"""

from __future__ import annotations

import argparse

from .config import DEFAULT_TIER, VOLUME_TIERS, GenConfig
from .dq_issues import inject
from .emit import write, write_batch
from .generate import generate
from .increment import FEEDS, init_state, tick
from .state import load_masters, load_state, make_backend, save_state


def _common(parser: argparse.ArgumentParser) -> None:
    parser.add_argument("--tier", choices=list(VOLUME_TIERS), default=DEFAULT_TIER)
    parser.add_argument("--seed", type=int, default=42)
    parser.add_argument("--out", default="out", help="output directory root")
    parser.add_argument("--state", default="state", help="state directory (§2a)")


def main(argv: list[str] | None = None) -> int:
    parser = argparse.ArgumentParser(prog="mockdata", description=__doc__)
    sub = parser.add_subparsers(dest="cmd", required=True)

    p_back = sub.add_parser("backfill", help="one-shot full generation (§2)")
    _common(p_back)
    p_back.add_argument("--snapshots", type=int, default=5)
    p_back.add_argument("--issue-rate", type=float, default=0.03)
    p_back.add_argument(
        "--no-issues",
        dest="inject_issues",
        action="store_false",
        help="emit clean data (no deliberate DQ issues)",
    )

    p_init = sub.add_parser("init", help="create state + emit reference loads (§2a)")
    _common(p_init)

    p_tick = sub.add_parser("tick", help="emit one incremental batch for a feed (§2a)")
    _common(p_tick)
    p_tick.add_argument("--feed", required=True, choices=list(FEEDS))
    p_tick.add_argument(
        "--count", type=int, default=None, help="batch size (orders/feedback)"
    )
    p_tick.add_argument(
        "--issues", action="store_true", help="inject row-level DQ issues"
    )
    p_tick.add_argument(
        "--volume-drop", action="store_true", help="(inventory) thin snapshot ~30%"
    )
    p_tick.add_argument(
        "--stale", action="store_true", help="(inventory) push load_ts +26h back"
    )
    p_tick.add_argument(
        "--schema-drift", action="store_true", help="(inventory) rename on_hand"
    )

    args = parser.parse_args(argv)
    config = GenConfig(
        tier=args.tier, seed=args.seed, out_dir=args.out, state_dir=args.state
    ).resolve()

    if args.cmd == "backfill":
        return _run_backfill(config, args)
    if args.cmd == "init":
        return _run_init(config)
    return _run_tick(config, args)


def _run_backfill(config: GenConfig, args: argparse.Namespace) -> int:
    config.inject_issues = args.inject_issues
    config.issue_rate = args.issue_rate
    config.snapshots = args.snapshots
    print(f"backfill tier={config.tier} seed={config.seed} -> {config.out_dir}/")
    datasets = generate(config)
    issues = inject(datasets, config) if config.inject_issues else []
    manifest = write(datasets, config, issues)
    total = sum(m["rows"] for m in manifest.values())
    print(f"  {len(manifest)} datasets, {total:,} rows")
    for name, m in manifest.items():
        print(
            f"    {name:<20} {m['rows']:>8,} rows  [{m['flow']}]  {len(m['files'])} file(s)"
        )
    if issues:
        print(
            f"  {len(issues)} deliberate DQ issues -> {config.out_dir}/_dq_issues_manifest.json"
        )
    return 0


def _run_init(config: GenConfig) -> int:
    print(f"init state={config.state_dir}/ tier={config.tier} seed={config.seed}")
    masters, _state = init_state(config)
    # Emit the reference/master loads once (Flow A Snowflake + UC reference data).
    manifest = write(masters, config, [])
    for name, m in manifest.items():
        print(f"    {name:<20} {m['rows']:>6,} rows  [{m['flow']}]")
    print(f"  state initialised -> {config.state_dir}/state.json (+ masters/)")
    print("  now drive ticks on a cadence, e.g.:")
    print("    python -m mockdata tick --feed orders     # 4x/day")
    print("    python -m mockdata tick --feed inventory  # daily")
    return 0


def _run_tick(config: GenConfig, args: argparse.Namespace) -> int:
    backend = make_backend(config.state_dir)
    state = load_state(backend)
    masters = load_masters(backend)
    datasets, issues = tick(
        config,
        state,
        masters,
        args.feed,
        args.count,
        inject_issues=args.issues,
        volume_drop=args.volume_drop,
        stale=args.stale,
        schema_drift=args.schema_drift,
    )
    batch_id = f"b{state.batch_counter:06d}"
    entry = write_batch(datasets, config, batch_id, args.feed, issues)
    save_state(backend, state)

    rows = sum(d["rows"] for d in entry["datasets"].values())
    print(
        f"tick #{state.batch_counter} feed={args.feed} batch={batch_id}: {rows:,} rows"
    )
    for name, d in entry["datasets"].items():
        print(f"    {name:<20} {d['rows']:>6,} rows -> {d['file']}")
    if args.feed in ("orders", "tracking"):
        print(f"    open shipments carried forward: {len(state.open_shipments)}")
    if issues:
        for it in issues:
            print(
                f"    issue: {it['kind']:<14} {it['dataset']:<20} n={it['n']} {it['detail']}"
            )
    return 0


if __name__ == "__main__":
    raise SystemExit(main())

    config.inject_issues = args.inject_issues
    config.issue_rate = args.issue_rate
    config.snapshots = args.snapshots
    print(f"backfill tier={config.tier} seed={config.seed} -> {config.out_dir}/")
    datasets = generate(config)
    issues = inject(datasets, config) if config.inject_issues else []
    manifest = write(datasets, config, issues)
    total = sum(m["rows"] for m in manifest.values())
    print(f"  {len(manifest)} datasets, {total:,} rows")
    for name, m in manifest.items():
        print(
            f"    {name:<20} {m['rows']:>8,} rows  [{m['flow']}]  {len(m['files'])} file(s)"
        )
    if issues:
        print(
            f"  {len(issues)} deliberate DQ issues -> {config.out_dir}/_dq_issues_manifest.json"
        )
    return 0


def _run_init(config: GenConfig) -> int:
    print(f"init state={config.state_dir}/ tier={config.tier} seed={config.seed}")
    masters, _state = init_state(config)
    # Emit the reference/master loads once (Flow A Snowflake + UC reference data).
    manifest = write(masters, config, [])
    for name, m in manifest.items():
        print(f"    {name:<20} {m['rows']:>6,} rows  [{m['flow']}]")
    print(f"  state initialised -> {config.state_dir}/state.json (+ masters/)")
    print("  now drive ticks on a cadence, e.g.:")
    print("    python -m mockdata tick --feed orders     # 4x/day")
    print("    python -m mockdata tick --feed inventory  # daily")
    return 0


def _run_tick(config: GenConfig, args: argparse.Namespace) -> int:
    store = make_store(config.state_dir)
    state = load_state(store)
    masters = load_masters(store)
    datasets, issues = tick(
        config,
        state,
        masters,
        args.feed,
        args.count,
        inject_issues=args.issues,
        volume_drop=args.volume_drop,
        stale=args.stale,
        schema_drift=args.schema_drift,
    )
    batch_id = f"b{state.batch_counter:06d}"
    entry = write_batch(datasets, config, batch_id, args.feed, issues)
    save_state(backend, state)

    rows = sum(d["rows"] for d in entry["datasets"].values())
    print(
        f"tick #{state.batch_counter} feed={args.feed} batch={batch_id}: {rows:,} rows"
    )
    for name, d in entry["datasets"].items():
        print(f"    {name:<20} {d['rows']:>6,} rows -> {d['file']}")
    if args.feed in ("orders", "tracking"):
        print(f"    open shipments carried forward: {len(state.open_shipments)}")
    if issues:
        for it in issues:
            print(
                f"    issue: {it['kind']:<14} {it['dataset']:<20} n={it['n']} {it['detail']}"
            )
    return 0


if __name__ == "__main__":
    raise SystemExit(main())

toolUseResult.originalFile:
"""Write generated datasets to a local out/ tree mirroring the §2 route map.

Layout (out/ root):
  snowflake/<table>/<table>.csv                 Flow A -> Snowflake (COPY INTO source)
  unity_catalog/<table>/<table>.parquet         Flow A -> UC plain load
  databricks_medallion/bronze/<src>/<src>.json  Flow B -> medallion bronze (NDJSON)
  adls_flatfile/logistics_tracking/tracking_events_<batch>.csv   Flow C (event-append)
  adls_flatfile/inventory_snapshot/inventory_<date>.parquet      Flow C (daily snapshot)

Two split datasets become multiple files:
  tracking_events  -> one CSV per `batch`        (same tracking_number recurs across files)
  inventory_snapshot -> one Parquet per `snapshot_date`

Schema-drift (the one cross-file deliberate issue) is applied here: the newest
inventory snapshot file renames on_hand -> qty_on_hand, appended to the DQ manifest.

Writes two sidecar manifests at the out/ root:
  _manifest.json            dataset -> {flow, format, files, rows}
  _dq_issues_manifest.json  the deliberate failures (row-level from dq_issues + schema drift)
"""

from __future__ import annotations

import json
from pathlib import Path

import pandas as pd

from .config import ROUTES, GenConfig


def write(
    datasets: dict[str, pd.DataFrame], config: GenConfig, dq_issues: list[dict]
) -> dict:
    root = Path(config.out_dir)
    root.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)
    file_manifest: dict[str, dict] = {}

    for name, df in datasets.items():
        route = ROUTES[name]
        target = root / route.subdir
        target.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)

        if name == "tracking_events":
            files = _write_tracking(df, target)
        elif name == "inventory_snapshot":
            files, drift = _write_inventory(df, target, config.inject_issues)
            if drift:
                dq_issues.append(drift)
        else:
            files = [_write_single(df, target, name, route.fmt)]

        file_manifest[name] = {
            "flow": route.flow,
            "format": route.fmt,
            "rows": len(df),
            "files": [str(p.relative_to(root)) for p in files],
        }

    (root / "_manifest.json").write_text(
        json.dumps(file_manifest, indent=2, default=str)
    )
    (root / "_dq_issues_manifest.json").write_text(
        json.dumps(dq_issues, indent=2, default=str)
    )
    return file_manifest


def _write_single(df: pd.DataFrame, target: Path, name: str, fmt: str) -> Path:
    path = target / f"{name}.{fmt}"
    _write_df(df, path, fmt)
    return path


def _write_df(df: pd.DataFrame, path: Path, fmt: str) -> None:
    if fmt == "csv":
        df.to_csv(path, index=False)
    elif fmt == "parquet":
        df.to_parquet(path, index=False)
    elif fmt == "json":  # NDJSON bronze raw
        df.to_json(path, orient="records", lines=True, date_format="iso")
    else:
        raise ValueError(f"unknown format {fmt!r}")


def write_batch(
    datasets: dict[str, pd.DataFrame],
    config: GenConfig,
    batch_id: str,
    feed: str,
    issues: list[dict],
) -> dict:
    """Write one incremental tick (§2a): batch-suffixed files into the route subdirs.

    ADLS landing zones are append-style (a new file per load), so each tick lands a
    distinct `<name>_<batch_id>.<fmt>` rather than overwriting. Appends one line to
    out/_ticks.jsonl and (per issue) out/_dq_issues_manifest.jsonl.
    """
    root = Path(config.out_dir)
    entry: dict = {"batch_id": batch_id, "feed": feed, "datasets": {}}
    for name, df in datasets.items():
        route = ROUTES[name]
        target = root / route.subdir
        target.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)
        path = target / f"{name}_{batch_id}.{route.fmt}"
        _write_df(df, path, route.fmt)
        entry["datasets"][name] = {
            "rows": len(df),
            "file": str(path.relative_to(root)),
        }

    with (root / "_ticks.jsonl").open("a") as f:
        f.write(json.dumps(entry, default=str) + "\n")
    if issues:
        with (root / "_dq_issues_manifest.jsonl").open("a") as f:
            for it in issues:
                f.write(json.dumps({**it, "batch_id": batch_id}, default=str) + "\n")
    return entry


def _write_tracking(df: pd.DataFrame, target: Path) -> list[Path]:
    paths = []
    for batch, group in df.groupby("batch"):
        path = target / f"tracking_events_{batch}.csv"
        group.drop(columns=["batch"]).to_csv(path, index=False)
        paths.append(path)
    return paths


def _write_inventory(
    df: pd.DataFrame, target: Path, inject_drift: bool
) -> tuple[list[Path], dict | None]:
    paths = []
    drift: dict | None = None
    latest_date = df["snapshot_date"].max()
    for snap_date, group in df.groupby("snapshot_date"):
        out = group.drop(columns=["snapshot_date"])
        if inject_drift and snap_date == latest_date:
            out = out.rename(
                columns={"on_hand": "qty_on_hand"}
            )  # schema drift on newest load
            drift = {
                "kind": "schema_drift",
                "dataset": "inventory_snapshot",
                "detail": f"newest snapshot {snap_date} renames on_hand -> qty_on_hand",
                "n": 1,
                "keys": [str(snap_date)],
            }
        path = target / f"inventory_{snap_date}.parquet"
        out.to_parquet(path, index=False)
        paths.append(path)
    return paths, drift

# DataQ harness — mock-data generator (§2 of HARNESS_TODO.md)

Believable, **referentially-consistent** retail data across the four DataQ run
paths, with **deliberate DQ issues seeded and manifested** so checks / severity /
alerting have something real to bite on (not just green dashboards).

Dependency-light: **pandas + pyarrow + stdlib only** (no Faker — stdlib `random`
+ curated pools give tighter control over the shared-key consistency §2 needs).

## Run

```bash
conda activate dataq            # has pandas + pyarrow
cd ~/Coding/Python/DataQ-harness
```

Three subcommands:

```bash
# §2  one-shot full backfill (masters + N snapshots + deliberate issues)
python -m mockdata backfill --tier S --seed 42 --out ./out

# §2a recurring cadence — init once, then tick each feed on a schedule
python -m mockdata init --out ./out --state ./state
python -m mockdata tick --feed orders     --out ./out --state ./state   # 4x/day
python -m mockdata tick --feed tracking   --out ./out --state ./state   # 2x/day
python -m mockdata tick --feed inventory  --out ./out --state ./state   # daily
python -m mockdata tick --feed feedback   --out ./out --state ./state   # every 2 days
```

Common flags: `--tier S/M/L/XL` (perf §6), `--seed`, `--out`, `--state`.
`backfill` adds `--snapshots N`, `--issue-rate`, `--no-issues`.
`tick` adds `--feed {orders,tracking,inventory,feedback}`, `--count N`
(orders/feedback batch size), `--issues` (row-level), and inventory anomaly flags
`--volume-drop` / `--stale` / `--schema-drift`.

> **Timestamps are anchored to generation wall-clock `now`** (offsets are seeded):
> the *latest* inventory snapshot and the tracking `delivered` batch are genuinely
> fresh, so the reserved **freshness** monitors (ADR 0012) bite. Everything else is
> reproducible per `--seed`.

## What it emits (the §2 route map)

| Dataset(s) | Flow / run path | Format |
|---|---|---|
| products, customers, orders_header, order_lines, payments, refunds, returns | **A — Snowflake** (custom-SQL, line-grain, reconciliation) | CSV |
| locations, channels, promotions | **A — Unity Catalog** plain load (reference data) | Parquet |
| sales_events, feedback | **B — Databricks medallion** bronze source | NDJSON |
| tracking_events (event-append) | **C — flat-file** in place (ADLS) | CSV per batch |
| inventory_snapshot (daily) | **C — flat-file** in place (ADLS) | Parquet per date |

Output tree mirrors the route map (`out/snowflake/…`, `out/unity_catalog/…`,
`out/databricks_medallion/bronze/…`, `out/adls_flatfile/…`). Two sidecar manifests
land at the `out/` root:

- `_manifest.json` — dataset → `{flow, format, files, rows}`
- `_dq_issues_manifest.json` — the deliberate failures, each with the affected `keys`

## Referential consistency (the hard part)

One system-of-record per entity; shared key pools mean the same id denotes the same
entity everywhere:

- `order_number` ties Orders ↔ Payments ↔ Refunds ↔ Returns ↔ Sales events ↔ Tracking
- `sku_id` ties Products ↔ Order lines ↔ Inventory ↔ Returns
- one `tracking_number` is stable across `ready → in_transit → delivered` batch files

So the cross-dataset reconciliation / FK-orphan / join checks measure **real** DQ —
the only inconsistencies are the deliberate ones.

## Reserved-monitor-kind shaping (ADR 0012/0014 — built post-v1, shaped now)

- **Freshness / volume / anomaly** → `inventory_snapshot` (timestamped `load_ts`,
  daily batches; one snapshot is thinned for a volume-band/anomaly hit)
- **Schema drift** → newest `inventory_snapshot` renames `on_hand → qty_on_hand`
- **Comparison / reconciliation** → in-dataset `sum(line_total)+tax == order_total`;
  cross-table `order_total ↔ payment.captured ↔ refund`; cross-platform
  Snowflake `order_total` ↔ Databricks `gold.daily_revenue` (sales_events roll-up)

## Deliberate DQ issues (default on)

nulls (channel, email) · duplicates (order_lines, product sku) · FK orphans
(`SKU-000000`, `CUST-000000`) · reconciliation mismatch (order_total, payment) ·
stale freshness (latest snapshot `load_ts` +26h back) · volume drop (mid snapshot
thinned to ~30%) · schema drift (newest snapshot). All recorded in
`_dq_issues_manifest.json` with affected keys.

## Recurring cadence (§2a)

`init` generates + persists the **master pools** (`state/masters/*.parquet`) and emits
the reference/warehouse loads once. Each `tick` then emits **one** batch-suffixed file
per dataset (`<name>_b000123.<fmt>` — ADLS landing zones are append-style) and advances
`state/state.json`:

- climbing id counters → `order_number` never collides across ticks
- an **open-shipment registry** → a `tracking_number` created on an orders tick gets its
  `ready_for_shipment → in_transit → delivered` events on *successive* tracking ticks
- `_ticks.jsonl` logs every batch; `_dq_issues_manifest.jsonl` appends per-tick issues

So referential consistency holds **across ticks**, not just within one run — the same
guarantee the backfill gives, extended over time.

**State backend.** `--state` accepts a local path *or* `adls://<account>/<filesystem>/<prefix>`
(`state.py` `StateBackend` seam: `LocalStateBackend` / `AdlsStateBackend`). The cron
**Container Apps Job** is ephemeral, so it points `--state` at ADLS blob (auth via
`DefaultAzureCredential` — managed identity in the Job, `az login` locally); local runs
default to the filesystem. Build/run the image via the repo-root `Dockerfile`.

**Cadence engine = Container Apps Job (cron)** for file creation; **Airflow/ADF** then
load + trigger DataQ (a mix of file-landing-event and scheduled-time triggers). cron
mapping: orders `0 0,6,12,18 * * *` · tracking `0 0,12 * * *` · inventory `0 1 * * *`
· feedback `0 1 */2 * *`.

## Next (§3)

`out/` is local. Uploading to the ADLS landing zone + COPY INTO Snowflake / load to
UC (and wiring the Airflow DAGs / ADF triggers that call these ticks) happens in §3
once Terraform (§1) is applied. The folder layout is already the landing-zone shape.

content.text:
Done. The `mockdata` image is now a fully self-contained Job.

## emit→ADLS output backend — done

Rather than duplicate the ADLS auth, I **factored the byte-storage primitive into a shared `storage.py`**:

- **`Store` seam** → `LocalStore` + `AdlsStore` + `make_store(uri)` — one ADLS-auth impl, now used by **both** `state.py` and `emit.py` (the old `state.py`-local backend classes were removed; it imports from `storage`).
- **`emit.py`** routes every write through the store: DataFrames → bytes via buffers (csv/parquet/json), manifests via `write_bytes` / `append_text` (jsonl). `--out adls://<acct>/<fs>[/<prefix>]` now writes the landing zone directly; a local path still writes the filesystem.

## Verified

- Local **backfill + init + tick** intact through the new store; ruff + black clean.
- In the rebuilt image: `make_store` routing correct (`local→LocalStore`, `adls://→AdlsStore`), prefix parsed, lazy azure import resolves, DataLake client constructs, and a write **reaches the Azure auth layer** — failing only with `ClientAuthenticationError` (no identity present). In the Job, managed identity supplies the token.

So both halves — state *and* output — now share one ADLS path, and a Job invocation is fully standalone (no mount):

```bash
python -m mockdata tick --feed inventory \
  --out adls://dataqharnessXXXX/landing --state adls://dataqharnessXXXX/landing/state
```

Tracker + README + memory updated. The only thing left for this layer is the **Container Apps Job Terraform** (`azurerm_container_app_job` + `schedule_trigger` + a registry for the image) — authorable now but apply-gated, so it slots in with §3.

That clears every **buildable-now** §2a item. The remaining harness work (Container Apps Job TF, the ADF/Airflow load+trigger wiring, §3 flows) is all apply-gated — so the **§1 `terraform apply`** (register the two RPs + greenlight) is now the single thing unblocking everything downstream. Want me to draft the Container Apps Job Terraform now (so it's ready the moment you apply), or pause here?

toolUseResult.stdout:
30:> Provisions the **datasources** (ADLS / Snowflake / UC). Airflow is **local Docker** (§3), so Terraform
31:> does NOT provision Airflow in the v1 profile. ADF is used in its managed cloud form.

lastPrompt: Where are we hosting the Airflow?

content.text:
Per the recorded plan: **local Docker on the dev box** — [HARNESS_TODO.md:30](HARNESS_TODO.md#L30) says *"Airflow is local Docker (§3); Terraform does NOT provision Airflow… ADF is used in its managed cloud form."* So the split is deliberate: **ADF = managed Azure** (the cloud-orchestrator reference), **Airflow = local docker-compose** (the second `OrchestrationProvider` impl, stood up disposably in §3).

That still works even though file-gen and the datasources are now in the cloud, because Airflow only needs to be a **network client**: a local scheduler can load cloud Snowflake/UC, read the ADLS landing zone, and POST the HMAC callback to DataQ's `/orchestration/events/airflow` webhook. DataQ doesn't care where Airflow runs — only that it gets the callback or can be polled.

**But two honest wrinkles surface from our recent cloud decisions** (worth being explicit since we just said "event/file-landing trigger for orders+tracking via Airflow"):

1. **No true push events to a laptop.** ADF gets native Event-Grid storage-event triggers; a *local* Airflow behind NAT can't receive an Event-Grid push, so its "file-landing" feeds use a **poll-based file sensor** (DAG waits on the file) instead of a push. Semantically still file-landing-triggered — just poll, not push. Fine for the harness, and it actually exercises DataQ's polling path nicely.
2. **The box must be running** for schedules to fire / sensors to poll. Acceptable for a demo; not "unattended in the cloud."

If you'd rather have Airflow always-on and receiving real events, the natural cloud upgrade — since we already provision an ADF factory — is **ADF Managed Airflow** (Azure runs Airflow as a feature of Data Factory; keeps both orchestrators under one roof). Alternatives: Airflow on Container Apps (reuse the platform) or a small always-on VM. All cost compute, vs. local Docker being free/disposable.

My recommendation: **keep local Docker for the harness** — it's sufficient to prove DataQ's Airflow integration (callback + polling fallback), matches the plan, and stays disposable. Treat ADF Managed Airflow as the opt-in upgrade only if you want the always-on / real-push behavior later.

Want me to leave it as local Docker, or switch the plan to ADF Managed Airflow (or one of the other cloud hosts)?

hookInfos.command: sh -c 'if ! command -v entire >/dev/null 2>&1; then exit 0; fi; exec entire hooks claude-code stop'
bash "${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT}/hooks/sg-python.sh" "${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT}/hooks/security_reminder_hook.py"

content.text: What will be the cost difference for ADF Managed Airflow vs Airflow on Container Apps?
Pricing changes, so let me ground this in current Microsoft docs rather than guess. Let me pull up the pricing tools.

{"results":[{"title":"Plan to manage costs for Azure Data Factory","content":"# Plan to manage costs for Azure Data Factory
## Understand the full billing model for Azure Data Factory
Azure Data Factory runs on Azure infrastructure that accrues costs when you deploy new resources. It's important to understand that other extra infrastructure costs might accrue.
### How you're charged for Azure Data Factory
Azure Data Factory is a serverless and elastic data integration service built for cloud scale. There isn't a fixed-size compute that you need to plan for peak load; rather you specify how much resource to allocate on demand per operation, which allows you to design the ETL processes in a much more scalable manner. In addition, ADF is billed on a consumption-based plan, which means you only pay for what you use.
When you create or use Azure Data Factory resources, you might get charged for the following meters:
1. Orchestration Activity Runs - You're charged for it based on the number of activity runs orchestrate.
2. Data Integration Unit (DIU) Hours - For copy activities run on Azure Integration Runtime, you're charged based on number of DIU used and execution duration.
3. vCore Hours - for data flow execution and debugging, you're charged for based on compute type, number of vCores, and execution duration.
At the end of your billing cycle, the charges for each meter are summed. Your bill or invoice shows a section for all Azure Data Factory costs. There's a separate line item for each meter.
### Other costs that might accrue with Azure Data Factory
When you create resources for Azure Data Factory (ADF), resources for other Azure services are also created. They include:
1. Pipeline Activity execution
2. External Pipeline Activity execution
3. Creation/editing/retrieving/monitoring of data factory artifacts
4. SSIS Integration Runtime (IR) duration based on instance type and duration
Note
You can assign the same tag to your ADF and other Azure resources, putting them into the same category to view their consolidated billing. ADF tag will be inherited by all SSIS IRs in it. If you change your ADF tag, you need to stop and restart all SSIS IRs in it for them to inherit the new tag, see [Reconfigure SSIS IR](https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/data-factory/manage-azure-ssis-integration-runtime#to-reconfigure-an-azure-ssis-ir) section.
### Using Azure Prepayment with Azure Data Factory
You can pay for Azure Data Factory charges with your Azure Prepayment credit. However, you can't use Azure Prepayment credit to pay for charges for third party products and services including those from the Azure Marketplace.","contentUrl":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/data-factory/plan-manage-costs#understand-the-full-billing-model-for-azure-data-factory"},{"title":"Plan to manage costs for Azure Data Factory","content":"# Plan to manage costs for Azure Data Factory
## Estimate costs before using Azure Data Factory
Use the [ADF pricing calculator](https://azure.microsoft.com/pricing/calculator/?service=data-factory) to get an estimate of the cost of running your ETL workload in Azure Data Factory. To use the calculator, you have to input details such as number of activity runs, number of data integration unit hours, type of compute used for Data Flow, core count, instance count, execution duration, and etc.
One of the commonly asked questions for the pricing calculator is what values should be used as inputs. During the proof-of-concept phase, you can conduct trial runs using sample datasets to understand the consumption for various ADF meters. Then based on the consumption for the sample dataset, you can project out the consumption for the full dataset and operational schedule.
Note
The prices used in this example below are hypothetical and are not intended to imply actual pricing.
For example, let’s say you need to move 1 TB of data daily from AWS S3 to Azure Data Lake Gen2. You can perform POC of moving 100 GB of data to measure the data ingestion throughput and understand the corresponding billing consumption.
Here's a sample copy activity run detail (your actual mileage will vary based on the shape of your specific dataset, network speeds, egress limits on S3 account, ingress limits on ADLS Gen2, and other factors).
![S3 copy run](https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/data-factory/media/plan-manage-costs/s3-copy-run-details.png)
By using the consumption monitoring at pipeline-run level, you can see the corresponding data movement meter consumption quantities:
![S3 copy pipeline consumption](https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/data-factory/media/plan-manage-costs/s3-copy-pipeline-consumption.png)
Therefore, the total number of DIU-hours it takes to move 1 TB per day for the entire month is:
1.2667 (DIU-hours) \* (1 TB / 100 GB) \* 30 (days in a month) = 380 DIU-hours
Now you can plug 30 activity runs and 380 DIU-hours into ADF pricing calculator to get an estimate of your monthly bill:
![S3 copy pricing calculator](https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/data-factory/media/plan-manage-costs/s3-copy-pricing-calculator.png)","contentUrl":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/data-factory/plan-manage-costs#estimate-costs-before-using-azure-data-factory"},{"title":"Applying FinOps to Azure Data Factory","content":"# Applying FinOps to Azure Data Factory
## Example scenarios
Estimated pricing for a month (8 hours per day for 30 days):
| Types | Calculation| 
|  --- | ---  |
| **Orchestration** (activity run counts in thousands) | 3 activity runs per execution (1 for trigger run, 2 for activity runs).Activity run counts/month = 3 \* 8 \* 30 = **720**.Activity run counts in thousand/month = **1** |
| **Execution** | 1. Data integration unit (DIU) hours: • DIU hours **per execution** = 10 min • Default DIU setting = 4 • DIU hours/month = (10 min / 60 min) \* 4 \* 8 \* 30 = **160**2. External pipeline activity execution hours: • Per execution time: 10 min • External pipeline activity execution hours = (10 min / 60 min) \* 8 \* 30 = **40** |

#### Pricing calculator example
Total scenario pricing for 30 days: $41.01
[![Screenshot of the Azure pricing calculator showing the cost estimation for this scenario.](https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/data-factory/media/apply-finops/pricing-calculator-scenario-1.png)](media/apply-finops/pricing-calculator-scenario-1.png#lightbox)
### Example: Using mapping data flow debug for a normal workday
This example shows mapping data flow debug costs for a typical workday for a data engineer. The prices used in the following example are hypothetical and aren't intended to imply exact actual pricing. Read/write and monitoring costs aren't shown since they're typically negligible and won't impact overall costs significantly. Activity runs are also rounded to the nearest 1000 in pricing calculator estimates.
#### Azure Data Factory engineer
A data factory engineer is responsible for designing, building, and testing mapping data flows every day. The engineer logs into the Azure Data Factory Studio in the morning and enables the debug mode for data Flows. The default Time to Live (TTL) for debug sessions is 60 minutes. The engineer works throughout the day for 8 hours, so the debug session never expires. Therefore, the engineer's charges for the day are:
8 hours \* 8 compute-optimized cores \* $0.193 per hour per core = **$12.35**","contentUrl":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/data-factory/apply-finops#example-scenarios"},{"title":"Apache Airflow compute in Fabric","content":"# Apache Airflow compute in Fabric
## Capacity units and pricing
Microsoft Fabric measures compute usage in **Capacity Units (CUs)**. All workloads, including Apache Airflow, use CUs.
For specifics about CUs and the pricing model for Apache Airflow pools, see [Apache Airflow job pricing for Data Factory in Microsoft Fabric](https://learn.microsoft.com/fabric/data-factory/pricing-apache-airflow-job).
For pricing specifics in your region, see [Microsoft Fabric pricing](https://azure.microsoft.com/pricing/details/microsoft-fabric/).
Tip
Custom pools don't shut down automatically when idle.
You pay for the base nodes (5 CUs for small, 10 CUs for large) as long as the pool is running, even if no jobs are running.
If you want to save costs during downtime, you can manually pause a custom pool or your whole Fabric capacity. When paused, the pool stops using CUs, but you can't run jobs until you resume it. Resuming takes a few minutes.","contentUrl":"https://learn.microsoft.com/fabric/data-factory/apache-airflow-compute#capacity-units-and-pricing"},{"title":"Save costs for resources with reserved capacity - Azure Data Factory data flows","content":"# Save costs for resources with reserved capacity - Azure Data Factory data flows
**APPLIES TO:**![](https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/data-factory/media/applies-to/yes.png) Azure Data Factory ![](https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/data-factory/media/applies-to/no.png) Azure Synapse Analytics
Tip
[Data Factory in Microsoft Fabric](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/fabric/data-factory) is the next generation of Azure Data Factory, with a simpler architecture, built-in AI, and new features. If you're new to data integration, start with Fabric Data Factory. Existing ADF workloads can upgrade to Fabric to access new capabilities across data science, real-time analytics, and reporting.
1. [Start a Fabric free trial](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/fabric/get-started/fabric-trial).
2. [Upgrade from Azure Data Factory to Data Factory in Microsoft Fabric](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/fabric/data-factory/migrate-planning-azure-data-factory)
Save money with Azure Data Factory data flow costs by committing to a reservation for compute resources compared to pay-as-you-go prices. With reserved capacity, you make a commitment for ADF data flow usage for a period of one or three years to get a significant discount on the compute costs. To purchase reserved capacity, you need to specify the Azure region, compute type, core count quantity, and term.
You do not need to assign the reservation to a specific factory or integration runtime. Existing factories or newly deployed factories automatically get the benefit. By purchasing a reservation, you commit to usage for the data flow compute costs for a period of one or three years. As soon as you buy a reservation, the compute charges that match the reservation attributes are no longer charged at the pay-as-you go rates.
You can buy [reserved capacity](https://portal.azure.com) by choosing reservations [up front or with monthly payments](https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/cost-management-billing/reservations/prepare-buy-reservation). To buy reserved capacity:
1. To buy a reservation, you must have owner role or reservation purchaser role on an Azure subscription.
2. For Enterprise subscriptions, **Add Reserved Instances** must be enabled in the [EA portal](https://ea.azure.com). Or, if that setting is disabled, you must be an EA Admin on the subscription. Reserved capacity.","contentUrl":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/data-factory/data-flow-reserved-capacity-overview"},{"title":"Applying FinOps to Azure Data Factory","content":"# Applying FinOps to Azure Data Factory
## Understanding Azure Data Factory pricing
The chart below explains the general flow of calculating data factory pricing. It shows how to use the [Azure Pricing Calculator](https://azure.microsoft.com/pricing/calculator/) for calculating pricing. Overall, the primary parts to understanding data factory billing involve these costs: **orchestration**, **execution**, **type of integration runtime (IR)**, **data movement (copy)**, and **data flows**.
1. Check whether your data factory source or sink integration runtime uses managed virtual network (VNET). If so, orchestration and execution are calculated using the Azure managed VNET IR. If not, proceed to the next step.
2. Confirm whether the source or sink uses the self-hosted integration runtime. If so, orchestration and execution are calculated by the self-hosted IR, and the total cost equals the sum of costs for both orchestration and execution. If not, orchestration and execution are calculated by the Azure IR.
3. For Azure IR and Azure managed VNET IR, confirm whether you use data flow. If so, the total cost equals the sum of costs for the data flow cluster, orchestration, and execution. Otherwise, the total cost is simply the sum of costs for orchestration and execution.
![Flow chart showing the process to calculate costs for Azure Data Factory.](https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/data-factory/media/apply-finops/pricing-flow-chart.png)","contentUrl":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/data-factory/apply-finops#understanding-azure-data-factory-pricing"},{"title":"Save costs for resources with reserved capacity - Azure Data Factory data flows","content":"# Save costs for resources with reserved capacity - Azure Data Factory data flows
For more information about how enterprise customers and Pay-As-You-Go customers are charged for reservation purchases, see [Understand Azure reservation usage for your Enterprise enrollment](https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/cost-management-billing/reservations/understand-reserved-instance-usage-ea) and [Understand Azure reservation usage for your Pay-As-You-Go subscription](https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/cost-management-billing/reservations/understand-reserved-instance-usage).
Note
Purchasing reserved capacity does not pre-allocate or reserve specific infrastructure resources (virtual machines or clusters) for your use.
## Determine proper Azure IR sizes needed before purchase
The size of reservation should be based on the total amount of compute used by the existing or soon-to-be-deployed data flows using the same compute tier.
For example, let's suppose that you are executing a pipeline hourly using memory optimized with 32 cores. Further, let's supposed that you plan to deploy within the next month an additional pipeline that uses general purpose 64 cores. Also, let's suppose that you know that you will need these resources for at least 1 year. In this case, enter the number of cores needed for each compute type for 1 hour. In the Azure portal, search for Reservations. Choose Data Factory > Data Flows, then enter 32 for memory optimized and 64 for general purpose.
## Buy reserved capacity
1. Sign in to the [Azure portal](https://portal.azure.com).
2. Select **All services** > **Reservations**.
3. Select **Add** and then in the **Purchase Reservations** pane, select **ADF Data Flows** to purchase a new reservation for ADF data flows.
4. Fill in the required fields and attributes you select qualify to get the reserved capacity discount. The actual number of data flows that get the discount depends on the scope and quantity selected.
5. Review the cost of the capacity reservation in the **Costs** section.
6. Select **Purchase**.
7. Select **View this Reservation** to see the status of your purchase.
## Cancel, exchange, or refund reservations
You can cancel, exchange, or refund reservations with certain limitations. For more information, see [Self-service exchanges and refunds for Azure Reservations](https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/cost-management-billing/reservations/exchange-and-refund-azure-reservations).
## Need help? Contact us
If you have questions or need help, [create a support request](https://portal.azure.com/#blade/Microsoft_Azure_Support/HelpAndSupportBlade/newsupportrequest).","contentUrl":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/data-factory/data-flow-reserved-capacity-overview"},{"title":"Plan to manage costs for Azure Data Factory","content":"# Plan to manage costs for Azure Data Factory
## Monitor costs
Following are known limitations of per pipeline billing features. These billing meters won't file under the pipeline that spins it, but instead will file under a fall-back line item for your factory.
1. Data Factory Operations charges, including Read/Write and Monitoring
2. Charges for [Azure Data Factory SQL Server Integration Services (SSIS) nodes](https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/data-factory/tutorial-deploy-ssis-packages-azure)
3. If you have [Time to Live (TTL)](https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/data-factory/concepts-integration-runtime-performance#time-to-live) configured for Azure Integration Runtime (Azure IR), Data Flow activities run on these IR won't file under individual pipelines.
### Monitor consumption at pipeline-run level in Azure Data Factory
Depending on the types of activities you have in your pipeline, how much data you're moving and transforming, and the complexity of the transformation, executing a pipeline will spin different billing meters in Azure Data Factory.
You can view the amount of consumption for different meters for individual pipeline runs in the Azure Data Factory user experience. To open the monitoring experience, select the **Monitor & Manage** tile in the data factory blade of the [Azure portal](https://portal.azure.com/). If you're already in the ADF UX, select on the **Monitor** icon on the left sidebar. The default monitoring view is list of pipeline runs.
Clicking the **Consumption** button next to the pipeline name will display a pop-up window showing you the consumption for your pipeline run aggregated across all of the activities within the pipeline.
![Pipeline run consumption](https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/data-factory/media/plan-manage-costs/pipeline-run-consumption.png)
![Pipeline consumption details](https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/data-factory/media/plan-manage-costs/pipeline-consumption-details.png)
The pipeline run consumption view shows you the amount consumed for each ADF meter for the specific pipeline run, but it doesn't show the actual price charged, because the amount billed to you is dependent on the type of Azure account you have and the type of currency used. To view the full list of supported account types, see [Understand Cost Management data](https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/cost-management-billing/costs/understand-cost-mgt-data).
### Monitor consumption at activity-run level in Azure Data Factory
Once you understand the aggregated consumption at pipeline-run level, there are scenarios where you need to further drill down and identify which is the most costly activity within the pipeline.","contentUrl":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/data-factory/plan-manage-costs#monitor-costs"},{"title":"Plan to manage costs for Azure Data Factory","content":"# Plan to manage costs for Azure Data Factory
## Monitor costs
In certain cases, you may want a granular breakdown of cost of operations within our factory, for instance, for charge back purposes. Integrating Azure Billing [cost analysis](https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/cost-management-billing/costs/quick-acm-cost-analysis) platform, Data Factory can separate out billing charges for each pipeline. By **opting in** Azure Data Factory detailed billing reporting for a factory, you can better understand how much each pipeline is costing you, within the aforementioned factory.
You need to opt in for *each* factory that you want detailed billing for. To turn on per pipeline detailed billing feature,
1. Go to Azure Data Factory portal
2. Under *Manage* tab, select *Factory setting* in *General* section
3. Select *Showing billing report* by pipeline
4. Publish the change
![Screenshot of steps to enable per pipeline billing feature.](https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/data-factory/media/plan-manage-costs/per-pipeline-enable.png)
Note
The detailed pipeline billing settings is *not* included in the exported ARM templates from your factory. That means [Continuous Integration and Delivery (CI/CD)](https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/data-factory/continuous-integration-delivery-improvements) will not overwrite billing behaviors for the factory. This allows you to set different billing behaviors for development, test, and production factories.
Once the feature is enabled, each pipeline will have a separate entry in our Billing report: It shows *exactly* how much each pipeline costs, in the selected time interval. It allows you to identify spending trends, and notice overspending, if any occurred.
![Screenshot of example showing granular cost for a pipeline.](https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/data-factory/media/plan-manage-costs/per-pipeline-billing-statement.png)
Using the graphing tools of Cost Analysis, you get similar charts and trends lines as shown above, but for individual pipelines. You also get the summary view by factory name, as factory name is included in billing report, allowing for proper filtering when necessary.
The change *only* impacts how bills are emitted going forward, and does not change past charges. Please give some time before the change populate to billing report: typically, the change is reflected within 1 day.
Warning
By opting in the per billing setting, there will be one entry for each pipeline in your factory. Please be particularly aware if you have excessive amount of pipelines in the factory, as it may significantly lengthen and complicate your billing report.
#### Limitations","contentUrl":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/data-factory/plan-manage-costs#monitor-costs"},{"title":"Applying FinOps to Azure Data Factory","content":"# Applying FinOps to Azure Data Factory
## Budgeting
When planning an Azure Data Factory implementation, it's important to understand and forecast your costs to help build a budget for your ETL and data integration projects.
![Screenshot of the pipeline monitoring screen highlighting the consumption report button.](https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/data-factory/media/apply-finops/pipeline-monitoring.png)
Select on the consumption report button from the pipeline monitoring view to get a snapshot of the units billed for each run.
On the monitoring page, you can manually use the consumption report for any pipeline run from a debug or manually triggered run, or even from an automated trigger run.
![Screenshot of the pipeline run consumption window showing the total units billed for a run.](https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/data-factory/media/apply-finops/pipeline-run-consumption.png)
The data factory pipeline consumption report provides the estimated units billed. You can run these tests using a debug execution of your pipeline on smaller datasets and then extrapolate your production budget from these estimates.
The consumption report provides values in units. To derive a monetary estimate from this, multiply the units’ value in this report by the price in your region based on the Azure pricing calculator. This produces an estimate for that pipeline execution. A best practice is to execute the pipeline several times with different datasets to get a baseline range of costs and use an average of those runs for your budgeting.","contentUrl":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/data-factory/apply-finops#budgeting"}]}
