The engineer logs into the Azure Data Factory Studio in the morning and enables the debug mode for data Flows. The default Time to Live (TTL) for debug sessions is 60 minutes. The engineer works throughout the day for 8 hours, so the debug session never expires. Therefore, the engineer's charges for the day are:
8 hours \* 8 compute-optimized cores \* $0.193 per hour per core = **$12.35**","contentUrl":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/data-factory/apply-finops#example-scenarios"},{"title":"Apache Airflow compute in Fabric","content":"# Apache Airflow compute in Fabric
## Capacity units and pricing
Microsoft Fabric measures compute usage in **Capacity Units (CUs)**. All workloads, including Apache Airflow, use CUs.
For specifics about CUs and the pricing model for Apache Airflow pools, see [Apache Airflow job pricing for Data Factory in Microsoft Fabric](https://learn.microsoft.com/fabric/data-factory/pricing-apache-airflow-job).
For pricing specifics in your region, see [Microsoft Fabric pricing](https://azure.microsoft.com/pricing/details/microsoft-fabric/).
Tip
Custom pools don't shut down automatically when idle.
You pay for the base nodes (5 CUs for small, 10 CUs for large) as long as the pool is running, even if no jobs are running.
If you want to save costs during downtime, you can manually pause a custom pool or your whole Fabric capacity. When paused, the pool stops using CUs, but you can't run jobs until you resume it. Resuming takes a few minutes.","contentUrl":"https://learn.microsoft.com/fabric/data-factory/apache-airflow-compute#capacity-units-and-pricing"},{"title":"Save costs for resources with reserved capacity - Azure Data Factory data flows","content":"# Save costs for resources with reserved capacity - Azure Data Factory data flows
**APPLIES TO:**![](https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/data-factory/media/applies-to/yes.png) Azure Data Factory ![](https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/data-factory/media/applies-to/no.png) Azure Synapse Analytics
Tip
[Data Factory in Microsoft Fabric](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/fabric/data-factory) is the next generation of Azure Data Factory, with a simpler architecture, built-in AI, and new features. If you're new to data integration, start with Fabric Data Factory. Existing ADF workloads can upgrade to Fabric to access new capabilities across data science, real-time analytics, and reporting.
1. [Start a Fabric free trial](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/fabric/get-started/fabric-trial).
2. [Upgrade from Azure Data Factory to Data Factory in Microsoft Fabric](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/fabric/data-factory/migrate-planning-azure-data-factory)
Save money with Azure Data Factory data flow costs by committing to a reservation for compute resources compared to pay-as-you-go prices. With reserved capacity, you make a commitment for ADF data flow usage for a period of one or three years to get a significant discount on the compute costs. To purchase reserved capacity, you need to specify the Azure region, compute type, core count quantity, and term.
You do not need to assign the reservation to a specific factory or integration runtime. Existing factories or newly deployed factories automatically get the benefit. By purchasing a reservation, you commit to usage for the data flow compute costs for a period of one or three years. As soon as you buy a reservation, the compute charges that match the reservation attributes are no longer charged at the pay-as-you go rates.
You can buy [reserved capacity](https://portal.azure.com) by choosing reservations [up front or with monthly payments](https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/cost-management-billing/reservations/prepare-buy-reservation). To buy reserved capacity:
1. To buy a reservation, you must have owner role or reservation purchaser role on an Azure subscription.
2. For Enterprise subscriptions, **Add Reserved Instances** must be enabled in the [EA portal](https://ea.azure.com). Or, if that setting is disabled, you must be an EA Admin on the subscription. Reserved capacity.","contentUrl":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/data-factory/data-flow-reserved-capacity-overview"},{"title":"Applying FinOps to Azure Data Factory","content":"# Applying FinOps to Azure Data Factory
## Understanding Azure Data Factory pricing
The chart below explains the general flow of calculating data factory pricing. It shows how to use the [Azure Pricing Calculator](https://azure.microsoft.com/pricing/calculator/) for calculating pricing. Overall, the primary parts to understanding data factory billing involve these costs: **orchestration**, **execution**, **type of integration runtime (IR)**, **data movement (copy)**, and **data flows**.
1. Check whether your data factory source or sink integration runtime uses managed virtual network (VNET). If so, orchestration and execution are calculated using the Azure managed VNET IR. If not, proceed to the next step.
2. Confirm whether the source or sink uses the self-hosted integration runtime. If so, orchestration and execution are calculated by the self-hosted IR, and the total cost equals the sum of costs for both orchestration and execution. If not, orchestration and execution are calculated by the Azure IR.
3. For Azure IR and Azure managed VNET IR, confirm whether you use data flow. If so, the total cost equals the sum of costs for the data flow cluster, orchestration, and execution. Otherwise, the total cost is simply the sum of costs for orchestration and execution.
![Flow chart showing the process to calculate costs for Azure Data Factory.](https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/data-factory/media/apply-finops/pricing-flow-chart.png)","contentUrl":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/data-factory/apply-finops#understanding-azure-data-factory-pricing"},{"title":"Save costs for resources with reserved capacity - Azure Data Factory data flows","content":"# Save costs for resources with reserved capacity - Azure Data Factory data flows
For more information about how enterprise customers and Pay-As-You-Go customers are charged for reservation purchases, see [Understand Azure reservation usage for your Enterprise enrollment](https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/cost-management-billing/reservations/understand-reserved-instance-usage-ea) and [Understand Azure reservation usage for your Pay-As-You-Go subscription](https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/cost-management-billing/reservations/understand-reserved-instance-usage).
Note
Purchasing reserved capacity does not pre-allocate or reserve specific infrastructure resources (virtual machines or clusters) for your use.
## Determine proper Azure IR sizes needed before purchase
The size of reservation should be based on the total amount of compute used by the existing or soon-to-be-deployed data flows using the same compute tier.
For example, let's suppose that you are executing a pipeline hourly using memory optimized with 32 cores. Further, let's supposed that you plan to deploy within the next month an additional pipeline that uses general purpose 64 cores. Also, let's suppose that you know that you will need these resources for at least 1 year. In this case, enter the number of cores needed for each compute type for 1 hour. In the Azure portal, search for Reservations. Choose Data Factory > Data Flows, then enter 32 for memory optimized and 64 for general purpose.
## Buy reserved capacity
1. Sign in to the [Azure portal](https://portal.azure.com).
2. Select **All services** > **Reservations**.
3. Select **Add** and then in the **Purchase Reservations** pane, select **ADF Data Flows** to purchase a new reservation for ADF data flows.
4. Fill in the required fields and attributes you select qualify to get the reserved capacity discount. The actual number of data flows that get the discount depends on the scope and quantity selected.
5. Review the cost of the capacity reservation in the **Costs** section.
6. Select **Purchase**.
7. Select **View this Reservation** to see the status of your purchase.
## Cancel, exchange, or refund reservations
You can cancel, exchange, or refund reservations with certain limitations. For more information, see [Self-service exchanges and refunds for Azure Reservations](https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/cost-management-billing/reservations/exchange-and-refund-azure-reservations).
## Need help? Contact us
If you have questions or need help, [create a support request](https://portal.azure.com/#blade/Microsoft_Azure_Support/HelpAndSupportBlade/newsupportrequest).","contentUrl":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/data-factory/data-flow-reserved-capacity-overview"},{"title":"Plan to manage costs for Azure Data Factory","content":"# Plan to manage costs for Azure Data Factory
## Monitor costs
Following are known limitations of per pipeline billing features. These billing meters won't file under the pipeline that spins it, but instead will file under a fall-back line item for your factory.
1. Data Factory Operations charges, including Read/Write and Monitoring
2. Charges for [Azure Data Factory SQL Server Integration Services (SSIS) nodes](https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/data-factory/tutorial-deploy-ssis-packages-azure)
3. If you have [Time to Live (TTL)](https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/data-factory/concepts-integration-runtime-performance#time-to-live) configured for Azure Integration Runtime (Azure IR), Data Flow activities run on these IR won't file under individual pipelines.
### Monitor consumption at pipeline-run level in Azure Data Factory
Depending on the types of activities you have in your pipeline, how much data you're moving and transforming, and the complexity of the transformation, executing a pipeline will spin different billing meters in Azure Data Factory.
You can view the amount of consumption for different meters for individual pipeline runs in the Azure Data Factory user experience. To open the monitoring experience, select the **Monitor & Manage** tile in the data factory blade of the [Azure portal](https://portal.azure.com/). If you're already in the ADF UX, select on the **Monitor** icon on the left sidebar. The default monitoring view is list of pipeline runs.
Clicking the **Consumption** button next to the pipeline name will display a pop-up window showing you the consumption for your pipeline run aggregated across all of the activities within the pipeline.
![Pipeline run consumption](https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/data-factory/media/plan-manage-costs/pipeline-run-consumption.png)
![Pipeline consumption details](https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/data-factory/media/plan-manage-costs/pipeline-consumption-details.png)
The pipeline run consumption view shows you the amount consumed for each ADF meter for the specific pipeline run, but it doesn't show the actual price charged, because the amount billed to you is dependent on the type of Azure account you have and the type of currency used. To view the full list of supported account types, see [Understand Cost Management data](https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/cost-management-billing/costs/understand-cost-mgt-data).
### Monitor consumption at activity-run level in Azure Data Factory
Once you understand the aggregated consumption at pipeline-run level, there are scenarios where you need to further drill down and identify which is the most costly activity within the pipeline.","contentUrl":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/data-factory/plan-manage-costs#monitor-costs"},{"title":"Plan to manage costs for Azure Data Factory","content":"# Plan to manage costs for Azure Data Factory
## Monitor costs
In certain cases, you may want a granular breakdown of cost of operations within our factory, for instance, for charge back purposes. Integrating Azure Billing [cost analysis](https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/cost-management-billing/costs/quick-acm-cost-analysis) platform, Data Factory can separate out billing charges for each pipeline. By **opting in** Azure Data Factory detailed billing reporting for a factory, you can better understand how much each pipeline is costing you, within the aforementioned factory.
You need to opt in for *each* factory that you want detailed billing for. To turn on per pipeline detailed billing feature,
1. Go to Azure Data Factory portal
2. Under *Manage* tab, select *Factory setting* in *General* section
3. Select *Showing billing report* by pipeline
4. Publish the change
![Screenshot of steps to enable per pipeline billing feature.](https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/data-factory/media/plan-manage-costs/per-pipeline-enable.png)
Note
The detailed pipeline billing settings is *not* included in the exported ARM templates from your factory. That means [Continuous Integration and Delivery (CI/CD)](https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/data-factory/continuous-integration-delivery-improvements) will not overwrite billing behaviors for the factory. This allows you to set different billing behaviors for development, test, and production factories.
Once the feature is enabled, each pipeline will have a separate entry in our Billing report: It shows *exactly* how much each pipeline costs, in the selected time interval. It allows you to identify spending trends, and notice overspending, if any occurred.
![Screenshot of example showing granular cost for a pipeline.](https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/data-factory/media/plan-manage-costs/per-pipeline-billing-statement.png)
Using the graphing tools of Cost Analysis, you get similar charts and trends lines as shown above, but for individual pipelines. You also get the summary view by factory name, as factory name is included in billing report, allowing for proper filtering when necessary.
The change *only* impacts how bills are emitted going forward, and does not change past charges. Please give some time before the change populate to billing report: typically, the change is reflected within 1 day.
Warning
By opting in the per billing setting, there will be one entry for each pipeline in your factory. Please be particularly aware if you have excessive amount of pipelines in the factory, as it may significantly lengthen and complicate your billing report.
#### Limitations","contentUrl":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/data-factory/plan-manage-costs#monitor-costs"},{"title":"Applying FinOps to Azure Data Factory","content":"# Applying FinOps to Azure Data Factory
## Budgeting
When planning an Azure Data Factory implementation, it's important to understand and forecast your costs to help build a budget for your ETL and data integration projects.
![Screenshot of the pipeline monitoring screen highlighting the consumption report button.](https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/data-factory/media/apply-finops/pipeline-monitoring.png)
Select on the consumption report button from the pipeline monitoring view to get a snapshot of the units billed for each run.
On the monitoring page, you can manually use the consumption report for any pipeline run from a debug or manually triggered run, or even from an automated trigger run.
![Screenshot of the pipeline run consumption window showing the total units billed for a run.](https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/data-factory/media/apply-finops/pipeline-run-consumption.png)
The data factory pipeline consumption report provides the estimated units billed. You can run these tests using a debug execution of your pipeline on smaller datasets and then extrapolate your production budget from these estimates.
The consumption report provides values in units. To derive a monetary estimate from this, multiply the units’ value in this report by the price in your region based on the Azure pricing calculator. This produces an estimate for that pipeline execution. A best practice is to execute the pipeline several times with different datasets to get a baseline range of costs and use an average of those runs for your budgeting.","contentUrl":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/data-factory/apply-finops#budgeting"}]}

results.title: Plan to manage costs for Azure Data Factory
results.content:
# Plan to manage costs for Azure Data Factory
## Understand the full billing model for Azure Data Factory
Azure Data Factory runs on Azure infrastructure that accrues costs when you deploy new resources. It's important to understand that other extra infrastructure costs might accrue.
### How you're charged for Azure Data Factory
Azure Data Factory is a serverless and elastic data integration service built for cloud scale. There isn't a fixed-size compute that you need to plan for peak load; rather you specify how much resource to allocate on demand per operation, which allows you to design the ETL processes in a much more scalable manner. In addition, ADF is billed on a consumption-based plan, which means you only pay for what you use.
When you create or use Azure Data Factory resources, you might get charged for the following meters:
1. Orchestration Activity Runs - You're charged for it based on the number of activity runs orchestrate.
2. Data Integration Unit (DIU) Hours - For copy activities run on Azure Integration Runtime, you're charged based on number of DIU used and execution duration.
3. vCore Hours - for data flow execution and debugging, you're charged for based on compute type, number of vCores, and execution duration.
At the end of your billing cycle, the charges for each meter are summed. Your bill or invoice shows a section for all Azure Data Factory costs. There's a separate line item for each meter.
### Other costs that might accrue with Azure Data Factory
When you create resources for Azure Data Factory (ADF), resources for other Azure services are also created. They include:
1. Pipeline Activity execution
2. External Pipeline Activity execution
3. Creation/editing/retrieving/monitoring of data factory artifacts
4. SSIS Integration Runtime (IR) duration based on instance type and duration
Note
You can assign the same tag to your ADF and other Azure resources, putting them into the same category to view their consolidated billing. ADF tag will be inherited by all SSIS IRs in it. If you change your ADF tag, you need to stop and restart all SSIS IRs in it for them to inherit the new tag, see [Reconfigure SSIS IR](https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/data-factory/manage-azure-ssis-integration-runtime#to-reconfigure-an-azure-ssis-ir) section.
### Using Azure Prepayment with Azure Data Factory
You can pay for Azure Data Factory charges with your Azure Prepayment credit. However, you can't use Azure Prepayment credit to pay for charges for third party products and services including those from the Azure Marketplace.

results.title: Plan to manage costs for Azure Data Factory
results.content:
# Plan to manage costs for Azure Data Factory
## Estimate costs before using Azure Data Factory
Use the [ADF pricing calculator](https://azure.microsoft.com/pricing/calculator/?service=data-factory) to get an estimate of the cost of running your ETL workload in Azure Data Factory. To use the calculator, you have to input details such as number of activity runs, number of data integration unit hours, type of compute used for Data Flow, core count, instance count, execution duration, and etc.
One of the commonly asked questions for the pricing calculator is what values should be used as inputs. During the proof-of-concept phase, you can conduct trial runs using sample datasets to understand the consumption for various ADF meters. Then based on the consumption for the sample dataset, you can project out the consumption for the full dataset and operational schedule.
Note
The prices used in this example below are hypothetical and are not intended to imply actual pricing.
For example, let’s say you need to move 1 TB of data daily from AWS S3 to Azure Data Lake Gen2. You can perform POC of moving 100 GB of data to measure the data ingestion throughput and understand the corresponding billing consumption.
Here's a sample copy activity run detail (your actual mileage will vary based on the shape of your specific dataset, network speeds, egress limits on S3 account, ingress limits on ADLS Gen2, and other factors).
![S3 copy run](https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/data-factory/media/plan-manage-costs/s3-copy-run-details.png)
By using the consumption monitoring at pipeline-run level, you can see the corresponding data movement meter consumption quantities:
![S3 copy pipeline consumption](https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/data-factory/media/plan-manage-costs/s3-copy-pipeline-consumption.png)
Therefore, the total number of DIU-hours it takes to move 1 TB per day for the entire month is:
1.2667 (DIU-hours) \* (1 TB / 100 GB) \* 30 (days in a month) = 380 DIU-hours
Now you can plug 30 activity runs and 380 DIU-hours into ADF pricing calculator to get an estimate of your monthly bill:
![S3 copy pricing calculator](https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/data-factory/media/plan-manage-costs/s3-copy-pricing-calculator.png)

results.title: Applying FinOps to Azure Data Factory
results.content:
# Applying FinOps to Azure Data Factory
## Example scenarios
Estimated pricing for a month (8 hours per day for 30 days):
| Types | Calculation| 
|  --- | ---  |
| **Orchestration** (activity run counts in thousands) | 3 activity runs per execution (1 for trigger run, 2 for activity runs).Activity run counts/month = 3 \* 8 \* 30 = **720**.Activity run counts in thousand/month = **1** |
| **Execution** | 1. Data integration unit (DIU) hours: • DIU hours **per execution** = 10 min • Default DIU setting = 4 • DIU hours/month = (10 min / 60 min) \* 4 \* 8 \* 30 = **160**2. External pipeline activity execution hours: • Per execution time: 10 min • External pipeline activity execution hours = (10 min / 60 min) \* 8 \* 30 = **40** |

#### Pricing calculator example
Total scenario pricing for 30 days: $41.01
[![Screenshot of the Azure pricing calculator showing the cost estimation for this scenario.](https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/data-factory/media/apply-finops/pricing-calculator-scenario-1.png)](media/apply-finops/pricing-calculator-scenario-1.png#lightbox)
### Example: Using mapping data flow debug for a normal workday
This example shows mapping data flow debug costs for a typical workday for a data engineer. The prices used in the following example are hypothetical and aren't intended to imply exact actual pricing. Read/write and monitoring costs aren't shown since they're typically negligible and won't impact overall costs significantly. Activity runs are also rounded to the nearest 1000 in pricing calculator estimates.
#### Azure Data Factory engineer
A data factory engineer is responsible for designing, building, and testing mapping data flows every day. The engineer logs into the Azure Data Factory Studio in the morning and enables the debug mode for data Flows. The default Time to Live (TTL) for debug sessions is 60 minutes. The engineer works throughout the day for 8 hours, so the debug session never expires. Therefore, the engineer's charges for the day are:
8 hours \* 8 compute-optimized cores \* $0.193 per hour per core = **$12.35**

results.title: Apache Airflow compute in Fabric
results.content:
# Apache Airflow compute in Fabric
## Capacity units and pricing
Microsoft Fabric measures compute usage in **Capacity Units (CUs)**. All workloads, including Apache Airflow, use CUs.
For specifics about CUs and the pricing model for Apache Airflow pools, see [Apache Airflow job pricing for Data Factory in Microsoft Fabric](https://learn.microsoft.com/fabric/data-factory/pricing-apache-airflow-job).
For pricing specifics in your region, see [Microsoft Fabric pricing](https://azure.microsoft.com/pricing/details/microsoft-fabric/).
Tip
Custom pools don't shut down automatically when idle.
You pay for the base nodes (5 CUs for small, 10 CUs for large) as long as the pool is running, even if no jobs are running.
If you want to save costs during downtime, you can manually pause a custom pool or your whole Fabric capacity. When paused, the pool stops using CUs, but you can't run jobs until you resume it. Resuming takes a few minutes.

In certain cases, you may want a granular breakdown of cost of operations within our factory, for instance, for charge back purposes. Integrating Azure Billing [cost analysis](https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/cost-management-billing/costs/quick-acm-cost-analysis) platform, Data Factory can separate out billing charges for each pipeline. By **opting in** Azure Data Factory detailed billing reporting for a factory, you can better understand how much each pipeline is costing you, within the aforementioned factory.
You need to opt in for *each* factory that you want detailed billing for. To turn on per pipeline detailed billing feature,
1. Go to Azure Data Factory portal
2. Under *Manage* tab, select *Factory setting* in *General* section
3. Select *Showing billing report* by pipeline
4. Publish the change
![Screenshot of steps to enable per pipeline billing feature.](https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/data-factory/media/plan-manage-costs/per-pipeline-enable.png)
Note
The detailed pipeline billing settings is *not* included in the exported ARM templates from your factory. That means [Continuous Integration and Delivery (CI/CD)](https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/data-factory/continuous-integration-delivery-improvements) will not overwrite billing behaviors for the factory. This allows you to set different billing behaviors for development, test, and production factories.
Once the feature is enabled, each pipeline will have a separate entry in our Billing report: It shows *exactly* how much each pipeline costs, in the selected time interval. It allows you to identify spending trends, and notice overspending, if any occurred.
![Screenshot of example showing granular cost for a pipeline.](https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/data-factory/media/plan-manage-costs/per-pipeline-billing-statement.png)
Using the graphing tools of Cost Analysis, you get similar charts and trends lines as shown above, but for individual pipelines. You also get the summary view by factory name, as factory name is included in billing report, allowing for proper filtering when necessary.
The change *only* impacts how bills are emitted going forward, and does not change past charges. Please give some time before the change populate to billing report: typically, the change is reflected within 1 day.
Warning
By opting in the per billing setting, there will be one entry for each pipeline in your factory. Please be particularly aware if you have excessive amount of pipelines in the factory, as it may significantly lengthen and complicate your billing report.
#### Limitations

results.content:
# Applying FinOps to Azure Data Factory
## Budgeting
When planning an Azure Data Factory implementation, it's important to understand and forecast your costs to help build a budget for your ETL and data integration projects.
![Screenshot of the pipeline monitoring screen highlighting the consumption report button.](https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/data-factory/media/apply-finops/pipeline-monitoring.png)
Select on the consumption report button from the pipeline monitoring view to get a snapshot of the units billed for each run.
On the monitoring page, you can manually use the consumption report for any pipeline run from a debug or manually triggered run, or even from an automated trigger run.
![Screenshot of the pipeline run consumption window showing the total units billed for a run.](https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/data-factory/media/apply-finops/pipeline-run-consumption.png)
The data factory pipeline consumption report provides the estimated units billed. You can run these tests using a debug execution of your pipeline on smaller datasets and then extrapolate your production budget from these estimates.
The consumption report provides values in units. To derive a monetary estimate from this, multiply the units’ value in this report by the price in your region based on the Azure pricing calculator. This produces an estimate for that pipeline execution. A best practice is to execute the pipeline several times with different datasets to get a baseline range of costs and use an average of those runs for your budgeting.

# Workflow Orchestration Manager pricing

**APPLIES TO:**![](media/applies-to/yes.png) Azure Data Factory ![](media/applies-to/no.png) Azure Synapse Analytics

Tip

Try out [Data Factory in Microsoft Fabric](/en-us/fabric/data-factory/), an all-in-one analytics solution for enterprises. [Microsoft Fabric](/en-us/fabric/get-started/microsoft-fabric-overview) covers everything from data movement to data science, real-time analytics, business intelligence, and reporting. Learn how to [start a new trial](/en-us/fabric/get-started/fabric-trial) for free!

Important

On January 1, 2026 you'll no longer be able to create new Airflow instances using ADF's Workflow Orchestration Manager. We recommend that you migrate all Workflow Orchestration Manager (Apache Airflow in Azure Data Factory) workloads to [Apache Airflow jobs in Microsoft Fabric](/en-us/fabric/data-factory/apache-airflow-jobs-concepts) before December 31, 2025.

For more information or for support during your migration to Apache Airflow in Microsoft Fabric, contact Microsoft Support.

This article describes the pricing for Workflow Orchestration Manager usage within Azure Data Factory.

## Pricing details

Workflow Orchestration Manager supports either small (D2v4) or large (D4v4) node sizing. Small can support up to 50 DAGs simultaneously, and large can support up to 1000 DAGs. The following table describes pricing for each option:

![Shows a screenshot of a table of pricing options for Workflow Orchestration Manager configuration.](media/airflow-pricing/airflow-pricing.png)

Based on the search results, here's the information about Azure Container Apps consumption plan pricing and free grants:

## Free Grant (Monthly)

The first 180,000 vCPU-seconds, 360,000 GiB-seconds, and 2 million requests per subscription per month are free.

## Pricing Model

Azure Container Apps consumption plan is billed based on per-second resource allocation and requests. Beyond the free grant, you pay for what you use on a per second basis based on the number of vCPU-s and GiB-s your applications are allocated.

## Active vs. Idle Rates

Based on recent pricing information from March 2026:
- Active vCPU-seconds are billed at $0.000024/vCPU-second while replicas are actively handling requests.
- Active GiB-seconds are billed at $0.000003/GiB-second alongside active CPU.
- When minimum replicas > 0, warm replicas are billed at reduced idle rates: $0.000008/vCPU-second and $0.000001/GiB-second.

## Request Charges

HTTP requests are billed at $0.40 per million requests after the 2 million free requests per month.

## Key Scaling Features

An application can be configured to scale to zero replicas when there are no requests or events to process. No usage charges apply when an application is scaled to zero.


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

content.text:
This turned up something that changes the answer: **ADF Managed Airflow is effectively off the table.**

Per the Microsoft docs, *"On January 1, 2026 you'll no longer be able to create new Airflow instances using ADF's Workflow Orchestration Manager"* — and today is 2026‑06‑26, so **we can't provision a new one.** Microsoft is steering everyone to **Apache Airflow jobs in Microsoft Fabric** instead. So my earlier "ADF Managed Airflow upgrade" suggestion is dead; the real cloud comparison is **Container Apps vs Fabric Airflow** (vs the current local‑Docker plan).

### Cost comparison (always‑on Airflow — scheduler can't scale to zero)

| Option | Model | Rough monthly cost | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Local Docker** (current plan) | your machine | **$0** | box must be running; poll‑based sensors, no Event‑Grid push |
| **Airflow on Container Apps** | Consumption: $0.000024/vCPU‑s active, **$0.000008 idle**; $0.000003/GiB‑s active, **$0.000001 idle**; 180k vCPU‑s + 360k GiB‑s free/mo | **~$25–75/mo** for 1 vCPU/2 GiB always‑on (+ ~$12–15/mo if you add a managed Postgres for Airflow metadata) | you operate scheduler+webserver+metadata DB; reuses the platform DataQ already runs on |
| **Fabric Apache Airflow jobs** (the ADF successor) | Capacity Units: base **5 CUs (small)/10 CUs (large)** billed continuously unless you pause; needs a Fabric capacity | **~$600+/mo** if never paused (≈5 CU × 730 h × ~$0.18/CU‑h — *approximate*, verify) | turnkey/managed, but heaviest cost; pausable to cut idle spend |
| ~~ADF Managed Airflow~~ | ~~$0.49/node‑h small, $0.99 large~~ | ~~~$358/mo+~~ | **can no longer be created (retired Jan 1 2026)** |

The Container Apps math (1 vCPU / 2 GiB, ~730 h/mo, after the free grant): at mostly‑idle warm rates ≈ **$25/mo**; at fully‑active 24/7 ≈ **$73/mo**. A harness scheduler mostly idles, so expect the low end.

### Takeaway

- **Container Apps is ~10× cheaper than the managed Fabric option** (~$25–75 vs ~$600+/mo) and reuses your existing platform — the trade‑off is you run Airflow yourself (scheduler + webserver + a small metadata Postgres) and it can't scale to zero.
- **Local Docker is still $0** and remains the sensible default for a *disposable* harness — it fully exercises DataQ's Airflow path (HMAC callback + polling).
- If you want cloud/always‑on, **Container Apps is now the cost‑effective choice** (Fabric only if you specifically want fully‑managed and accept the cost).

Numbers are estimates — real bills depend on sizing, region, active/idle split, and the metadata‑DB choice; I computed the Container Apps figures from the documented unit rates.

Want me to record this (ADF Managed Airflow retired → Container Apps as the cloud option if we move off local Docker) in the tracker, and keep the plan as local Docker for now?

Sources:
- [Workflow Orchestration Manager (ADF Managed Airflow) pricing + retirement notice — Microsoft Learn](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/data-factory/airflow-pricing)
- [Apache Airflow compute in Fabric — capacity units & pricing](https://learn.microsoft.com/fabric/data-factory/apache-airflow-compute)
- [Azure Container Apps pricing](https://azure.microsoft.com/en-us/pricing/details/container-apps/) · [Billing in Azure Container Apps](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/container-apps/billing)

---

## 1. Terraform — cloud infra bootstrap (external, not git-tracked)

> Provisions the **datasources** (ADLS / Snowflake / UC). Airflow is **local Docker** (§3), so Terraform
> does NOT provision Airflow in the v1 profile. ADF is used in its managed cloud form.

- [x] `terraform/` skeleton in the harness root with backend (local state, gitignored) + provider blocks (azurerm 4.x, snowflakedb/snowflake 1.x, databricks 1.x, random) — secret-free, creds from env
- [~] **ADLS Gen2**: storage account (HNS) + `landing`/`raw` filesystems — authored (`adls.tf`), in the clean plan, not yet applied
- [~] **Snowflake**: `DATAQ_DB` + `RETAIL` schema + `DATAQ_WH` warehouse + least-priv `DATAQ_READER` role (granted to existing user — no new password) — authored (`snowflake.tf`), in plan, not applied
- [~] **Unity Catalog**: `dataq_retail` catalog + raw/silver/gold/reference schemas + grants to PAT user — authored (`unity_catalog.tf`), in plan, not applied
- [~] **ADF**: `dataq-harness-adf` data factory (SystemAssigned identity) — authored (`adf.tf`), in plan, not applied
- [x] Outputs: connection coordinates (`outputs.tf`) — ADLS account/DFS endpoint/filesystems, ADF name, Snowflake db/wh/role/schemas, UC catalog/schemas (no secrets emitted)
- [ ] `terraform apply` clean; record outputs into the harness `.env` (local only) — **BLOCKED on apply decision + subscription RP registration** (`Microsoft.Storage`, `Microsoft.DataFactory` — SP can't self-register; needs an Owner; see terraform/README.md). `terraform plan` is **clean: 21 to add, 0 change, 0 destroy** (all 3 providers authenticate).
- [ ] Teardown verified (`terraform destroy`) so the demo env is disposable
- [x] **No Azure resource names leak into the DataQ repo** (ADR 0010/0013) — names live only in the harness `terraform/` (outside the repo, gitignored)

- [~] **ADF**: `dataq-harness-adf` data factory (SystemAssigned identity) — authored (`adf.tf`), in plan, not applied
- [ ] **Container Apps Environment** (`azurerm_container_app_environment`) — shared host for Airflow + the mockdata Job. Needs `Microsoft.App` (+ `Microsoft.OperationalInsights` for the Log Analytics workspace) RPs registered
- [ ] **Container registry** (`azurerm_container_registry`, Basic) — holds the `mockdata` image + the Airflow image; Environment/Job/App pull via managed identity (AcrPull)
- [ ] **Postgres** for Airflow metadata (`azurerm_postgresql_flexible_server`, Burstable B1ms — cheapest) — or co-locate; password generated locally, never committed
- [ ] **Airflow on Container Apps** (`azurerm_container_app`) — scheduler+webserver (LocalExecutor to start), min replicas ≥1 (scheduler can't scale to zero), HTTPS ingress (so Event-Grid/DataQ can reach it). Image built + pushed to the registry. ~$25–75/mo always-on (see §2a cost note)
- [x] Outputs: connection coordinates (`outputs.tf`) — ADLS account/DFS endpoint/filesystems, ADF name, Snowflake db/wh/role/schemas, UC catalog/schemas (no secrets emitted)

- [x] **emit output backend → ADLS** — factored the byte-storage primitive into a shared `storage.py` `Store` seam (`LocalStore` + `AdlsStore` + `make_store`, used by **both** `state.py` and `emit.py` — one ADLS-auth impl, no dup). `emit.py` routes every write (csv/parquet/json via buffers + jsonl `append_text`) through the store; `--out adls://<acct>/<fs>[/<prefix>]` now writes the landing zone directly. **Verified:** local backfill+tick intact; ruff+black clean; in the image, `make_store` routing + lazy azure import + DataLake client construct, and a write reaches the Azure auth layer (fails only on missing identity — managed identity supplies it in the Job). **Job is now fully standalone** (no mount needed); only live ADLS write awaits apply

input.old_string: **Cadence decisions:** file creation = **Container Apps Job (cron)** running the image (source-system simulator, upstream); load+trigger = **ADF/Airflow** with a *mix* of file-landing-event (orders/tracking) and scheduled-time (inventory/feedback) triggers → DataQ. **Airflow hosting = Azure Container Apps** (decided 2026-06-26), NOT local Docker, NOT ADF Managed Airflow (Microsoft retired new-instance creation 2026-01-01 → successor is Fabric Airflow). content.text:
Done — recorded across the tracker and memory. Here's what the decision changes:

**§1 Terraform grows** (all apply-gated): a shared **Container Apps Environment** hosting both Airflow (scheduler+webserver, LocalExecutor, min‑replicas ≥1, HTTPS ingress) *and* the mockdata Job, plus a **container registry** (both images) and a small **Postgres B1ms** for Airflow metadata. ADF stays managed-cloud.

**A nice bonus:** cloud Airflow has a public HTTPS ingress, so it can receive **real Event‑Grid push** for the file-landing feeds — the local-laptop "poll-only" wrinkle disappears. Event triggers can now be true push.

**One thing to flag — the apply prerequisite list expands.** The Terraform SP can't self-register subscription RPs (we hit that in the plan), so before apply an Owner now needs to register, on top of `Microsoft.Storage` + `Microsoft.DataFactory`:
- `Microsoft.App` (Container Apps)
- `Microsoft.OperationalInsights` (Log Analytics the environment needs)
- `Microsoft.ContainerRegistry`
- `Microsoft.DBforPostgreSQL`

I'll fold that into the terraform README when I author these resources.

**Cost reminder:** ~$25–75/mo for the always-on Airflow (1 vCPU/2 GiB) + ~$12–15/mo for the Postgres — vs $0 local Docker and ~$600+/mo Fabric. The disposable `terraform destroy` keeps it bounded.

Buildable-now from here: the **Airflow image** (Dockerfile + DAG skeleton calling the tick CLI / load steps) and the **Container Apps Terraform** (environment + registry + Postgres + Airflow app + the mockdata Job) — authorable now so it's ready the moment you apply, though plan/apply themselves wait on the RP registration.

Want me to author the Container Apps Terraform (environment + registry + Postgres + Airflow app + mockdata Job) now, or pause here?

input.content:
# Container Apps platform — shared host for Airflow (airflow.tf) + the mockdata
# cron Jobs (mockdata_job.tf). Decision 2026-06-26: Airflow on Container Apps
# (not local Docker; ADF Managed Airflow was retired for new instances 2026-01-01).
# Needs subscription RPs: Microsoft.App, Microsoft.OperationalInsights,
# Microsoft.ContainerRegistry (registered by an Owner — see README).

resource "azurerm_log_analytics_workspace" "harness" {
  name                = "dataq-harness-logs"
  location            = var.azure_location
  resource_group_name = data.azurerm_resource_group.dataq.name
  sku                 = "PerGB2018"
  retention_in_days   = var.log_retention_days
  tags                = local.common_tags
}

resource "azurerm_container_app_environment" "harness" {
  name                       = "dataq-harness-cae"
  location                   = var.azure_location
  resource_group_name        = data.azurerm_resource_group.dataq.name
  log_analytics_workspace_id = azurerm_log_analytics_workspace.harness.id
  tags                       = local.common_tags
}

# Holds the mockdata image (+ a custom Airflow image once §3 bakes DAGs in).
# admin_enabled = true so the Job pulls with registry creds — avoids an AcrPull
# role assignment (the Terraform SP is Contributor-only and can't grant roles).
resource "azurerm_container_registry" "harness" {
  name                = "${var.acr_prefix}${random_string.suffix.result}"
  resource_group_name = data.azurerm_resource_group.dataq.name
  location            = var.azure_location
  sku                 = "Basic"
  admin_enabled       = true
  tags                = local.common_tags
}

input.content:
# Airflow metadata database — Postgres Flexible Server (Burstable B1ms, cheapest).
# Public access + "allow Azure services" so the Container Apps Environment reaches it
# without VNet integration (fine for a disposable harness). Password generated locally
# (random_password) — never committed; state is gitignored.

resource "random_password" "pg" {
  length  = 24
  special = false # keep the SQLAlchemy URL clean (no %-encoding)
}

resource "azurerm_postgresql_flexible_server" "airflow" {
  name                          = "dataq-airflow-pg-${random_string.suffix.result}"
  resource_group_name           = data.azurerm_resource_group.dataq.name
  location                      = var.azure_location
  version                       = "16"
  administrator_login           = var.postgres_admin_login
  administrator_password        = random_password.pg.result
  sku_name                      = var.postgres_sku
  storage_mb                    = 32768
  auto_grow_enabled             = true
  public_network_access_enabled = true
  zone                          = "1"
  tags                          = local.common_tags
}

resource "azurerm_postgresql_flexible_server_database" "airflow" {
  name      = "airflow"
  server_id = azurerm_postgresql_flexible_server.airflow.id
  collation = "en_US.utf8"
  charset   = "utf8"
}

# start=end=0.0.0.0 is Azure's special "allow access from Azure services" rule.
resource "azurerm_postgresql_flexible_server_firewall_rule" "azure_services" {
  name             = "allow-azure-services"
  server_id        = azurerm_postgresql_flexible_server.airflow.id
  start_ip_address = "0.0.0.0"
  end_ip_address   = "0.0.0.0"
}

class AdlsStore(Store):
    """ADLS Gen2 store for the Container Apps Job.

    Auth via DefaultAzureCredential — managed identity in the Job, `az login` (or
    AZURE_* env SP) locally. azure-* libs are imported lazily so the package runs
    with the local store even when they aren't installed.
    """

    def __init__(self, account: str, filesystem: str, prefix: str) -> None:
        from azure.identity import DefaultAzureCredential
        from azure.storage.filedatalake import DataLakeServiceClient

        self.prefix = prefix.strip("/")
        svc = DataLakeServiceClient(
            account_url=f"https://{account}.dfs.core.windows.net",
            credential=DefaultAzureCredential(),
        )
        self._fs = svc.get_file_system_client(filesystem)

class AdlsStore(Store):
    """ADLS Gen2 store for the Container Apps Job.

    Auth: if AZURE_STORAGE_CONNECTION_STRING is set (the Job passes the storage
    account connection string — avoids a role assignment the Contributor-only
    Terraform SP can't grant), use it; otherwise DefaultAzureCredential (managed
    identity in-cloud, `az login` locally). azure-* libs are imported lazily so the
    package runs with the local store even when they aren't installed.
    """

    def __init__(self, account: str, filesystem: str, prefix: str) -> None:
        import os

        from azure.storage.filedatalake import DataLakeServiceClient

        self.prefix = prefix.strip("/")
        conn = os.environ.get("AZURE_STORAGE_CONNECTION_STRING")
        if conn:
            svc = DataLakeServiceClient.from_connection_string(conn)
        else:
            from azure.identity import DefaultAzureCredential

            svc = DataLakeServiceClient(
                account_url=f"https://{account}.dfs.core.windows.net",
                credential=DefaultAzureCredential(),
            )
        self._fs = svc.get_file_system_client(filesystem)

# DataQ harness — Terraform (§1 of HARNESS_TODO.md)

Provisions the **datasources** for the live test/demo-data environment (ADR 0021):
ADLS Gen2 landing zone, Snowflake DB/warehouse/reader-role, Unity Catalog
catalog+schemas, and the ADF factory. Airflow is local Docker (§3), not provisioned here.

**Secret-free by design:** every provider reads its credentials from the environment
exported by `secrets.sh`. No passwords/keys/tokens live in `.tf` files or outputs.

## Usage

```bash
cd ~/Coding/Python/DataQ-harness
source secrets.sh                 # exports ARM_* / SNOWFLAKE_* / DATABRICKS_* 
cd terraform
terraform init
terraform validate
terraform plan                    # review — creates nothing
terraform apply                   # provisions real, billable resources
```

### Apply-time prerequisite — subscription resource providers

The Terraform SP is Contributor on `dataq-rg` only, so it **cannot register
subscription resource providers** (`resource_provider_registrations = "none"`).
Before the first `apply`, a subscription **Owner** must register the RPs the
harness uses:

```bash
az provider register --namespace Microsoft.Storage
az provider register --namespace Microsoft.DataFactory
```

`terraform plan` passes without this (it only reads the RG); `apply` of the
storage account / data factory will 403 if the RPs aren't registered.

After `apply`, copy outputs back into `secrets.sh` blanks:
`SNOWFLAKE_DATABASE`, `SNOWFLAKE_WAREHOUSE`, `ADF_FACTORY_NAME` (and switch
`SNOWFLAKE_ROLE` to the reader role once Flow A has loaded tables).

```bash
terraform output                  # connection coordinates for DataQ's connection editor
```

## Teardown (the env is disposable)

```bash
terraform destroy
```

## What's where

| File | Resources |
|---|---|
| `versions.tf`       | Terraform + provider pins, local state backend |
| `providers.tf`      | Provider blocks (creds from env) |
| `variables.tf`      | Non-secret config (regions, names) |
| `main.tf`           | RG data source, random suffix, common tags |
| `adls.tf`           | ADLS Gen2 account + `landing`/`raw` filesystems |
| `snowflake.tf`      | DB + schema(s) + warehouse + least-priv reader role/grants |
| `unity_catalog.tf`  | UC catalog + raw/silver/gold/reference schemas + grants |
| `adf.tf`            | Data Factory instance (Flow A pipelines authored later) |
| `outputs.tf`        | Connection coordinates (no secrets) |

> **No Azure resource names leak into the DataQ repo** (ADR 0010/0013). They live only here.

input.new_string:
```bash
az provider register --namespace Microsoft.Storage
az provider register --namespace Microsoft.DataFactory
az provider register --namespace Microsoft.App                # Container Apps
az provider register --namespace Microsoft.OperationalInsights # Log Analytics
az provider register --namespace Microsoft.ContainerRegistry
az provider register --namespace Microsoft.DBforPostgreSQL
```

`terraform plan`/`validate` pass without this (they only read the RG); `apply` will
403 on the matching resources if the RPs aren't registered.

### Post-apply

```bash
terraform output                  # connection coordinates + airflow_url + acr_login_server
terraform output -raw airflow_admin_password   # Airflow web login (user = airflow_admin_user)
```

1. Copy outputs into `secrets.sh` blanks: `SNOWFLAKE_DATABASE`, `SNOWFLAKE_WAREHOUSE`,
   `ADF_FACTORY_NAME` (switch `SNOWFLAKE_ROLE` to the reader role once Flow A has loaded tables).
2. **Build + push the mockdata image** (the cron Jobs reference it):
   ```bash
   ACR=$(terraform output -raw acr_login_server)
   az acr login -n "${ACR%%.*}"
   (cd .. && docker build -t "$ACR/mockdata:latest" . && docker push "$ACR/mockdata:latest")
   ```
3. **Seed state once** — run `init` (locally against ADLS, or as a one-off Job exec):
   `python -m mockdata init --out adls://<acct>/landing --state adls://<acct>/landing/state`
   (set `AZURE_STORAGE_CONNECTION_STRING` or `az login` first). The cron Jobs then tick.

| `container_apps.tf` | Log Analytics + Container Apps Environment + container registry |
| `postgres.tf`       | Airflow metadata Postgres (Burstable B1ms) + db + firewall |

toolUseResult.originalFile:
# DataQ harness — Terraform (§1 of HARNESS_TODO.md)

Provisions the **datasources** for the live test/demo-data environment (ADR 0021):
ADLS Gen2 landing zone, Snowflake DB/warehouse/reader-role, Unity Catalog
catalog+schemas, the ADF factory — **and the compute platform**: a shared Container
Apps Environment hosting Airflow (scheduler+webserver + Postgres metadata) and the
mockdata cron Jobs (§2a), behind a container registry. (ADF Managed Airflow was retired
for new instances 2026-01-01, so Airflow runs on Container Apps.)

**Secret-free by design:** every provider reads its credentials from the environment
exported by `secrets.sh`. No passwords/keys/tokens live in `.tf` files or outputs.

## Usage

```bash
cd ~/Coding/Python/DataQ-harness
source secrets.sh                 # exports ARM_* / SNOWFLAKE_* / DATABRICKS_* 
cd terraform
terraform init
terraform validate
terraform plan                    # review — creates nothing
terraform apply                   # provisions real, billable resources
```

### Apply-time prerequisite — subscription resource providers

The Terraform SP is Contributor on `dataq-rg` only, so it **cannot register
subscription resource providers** (`resource_provider_registrations = "none"`).
Before the first `apply`, a subscription **Owner** must register the RPs the
harness uses:

```bash
az provider register --namespace Microsoft.Storage
az provider register --namespace Microsoft.DataFactory
az provider register --namespace Microsoft.App                # Container Apps
az provider register --namespace Microsoft.OperationalInsights # Log Analytics
az provider register --namespace Microsoft.ContainerRegistry
az provider register --namespace Microsoft.DBforPostgreSQL
```

`terraform plan`/`validate` pass without this (they only read the RG); `apply` will
403 on the matching resources if the RPs aren't registered.

### Post-apply

```bash
terraform output                  # connection coordinates + airflow_url + acr_login_server
terraform output -raw airflow_admin_password   # Airflow web login (user = airflow_admin_user)
```

1. Copy outputs into `secrets.sh` blanks: `SNOWFLAKE_DATABASE`, `SNOWFLAKE_WAREHOUSE`,
   `ADF_FACTORY_NAME` (switch `SNOWFLAKE_ROLE` to the reader role once Flow A has loaded tables).
2. **Build + push the mockdata image** (the cron Jobs reference it):
   ```bash
   ACR=$(terraform output -raw acr_login_server)
   az acr login -n "${ACR%%.*}"
   (cd .. && docker build -t "$ACR/mockdata:latest" . && docker push "$ACR/mockdata:latest")
   ```
3. **Seed state once** — run `init` (locally against ADLS, or as a one-off Job exec):
   `python -m mockdata init --out adls://<acct>/landing --state adls://<acct>/landing/state`
   (set `AZURE_STORAGE_CONNECTION_STRING` or `az login` first). The cron Jobs then tick.

## Teardown (the env is disposable)

```bash
terraform destroy
```

## What's where

| File | Resources |
|---|---|
| `versions.tf`       | Terraform + provider pins, local state backend |
| `providers.tf`      | Provider blocks (creds from env) |
| `variables.tf`      | Non-secret config (regions, names) |
| `main.tf`           | RG data source, random suffix, common tags |
| `adls.tf`           | ADLS Gen2 account + `landing`/`raw` filesystems |
| `snowflake.tf`      | DB + schema(s) + warehouse + least-priv reader role/grants |
| `unity_catalog.tf`  | UC catalog + raw/silver/gold/reference schemas + grants |
| `adf.tf`            | Data Factory instance (Flow A pipelines authored later) |
| `outputs.tf`        | Connection coordinates (no secrets) |

> **No Azure resource names leak into the DataQ repo** (ADR 0010/0013). They live only here.

content.text:
Done — the full Airflow-on-Container-Apps stack is authored and `terraform validate`-clean.

## What I added (`terraform/`)

| File | Resources |
|---|---|
| `container_apps.tf` | Log Analytics workspace + **Container Apps Environment** (shared) + **container registry** (Basic, admin-enabled) |
| `postgres.tf` | **Airflow metadata Postgres** (Flexible Server B1ms) + `airflow` db + allow-Azure firewall; password via `random_password` |
| `airflow.tf` | **Airflow Container App** — init-container (`db migrate` + admin user), scheduler + webserver containers (LocalExecutor), `min_replicas=1`, external `:8080` ingress; Fernet/webserver/admin secrets generated |
| `mockdata_job.tf` | **mockdata cron Jobs** — one `azurerm_container_app_job` per feed over `var.feed_crons`, `tick --feed <name>` → `--out/--state adls://…` |
| `variables.tf` / `outputs.tf` | new vars (image, crons, pg, registry) + outputs (`airflow_url`, `acr_login_server`, `postgres_fqdn`, sensitive admin password, job map) |

## Key design calls (so apply works under the Contributor-only SP)

- **No role assignments** (the SP can't grant RBAC): registry pull uses **admin creds**; the Job writes ADLS via the **storage connection string** — I extended `AdlsStore` to use `AZURE_STORAGE_CONNECTION_STRING` when set, else fall back to managed identity. Both documented as swappable to managed-identity if you apply as an Owner.
- **Valid Fernet key generated in TF** (`random_id` → url-safe base64) — no secret in files, self-contained apply.
- **Airflow starts on the public `apache/airflow:2.10.5` image** (no DAGs yet); §3 swaps `airflow_image` for a registry image with DAGs baked in.

## Verified

- `terraform fmt` + **`terraform validate` clean** (all new schemas resolve — container app with init-container/dynamic-env, container app job with `schedule_trigger_config`, Postgres flexible server).
- `storage.py` change didn't disturb the local path: `init`+`tick` still run, ruff+black clean.

## Apply prerequisites grew (in the README)

Owner must now register **6 RPs**: `Microsoft.Storage`, `Microsoft.DataFactory`, `Microsoft.App`, `Microsoft.OperationalInsights`, `Microsoft.ContainerRegistry`, `Microsoft.DBforPostgreSQL`. Post-apply flow documented: copy outputs → build+push the mockdata image → `init` state once → cron Jobs tick.

Everything here is authored/validate-clean but **apply-gated** (RPs + your greenlight). Remaining harness work is now all post-apply: the **§3 reference flows** + the Airflow **DAGs** (and swapping in the custom Airflow image). Want me to scaffold the Airflow DAG skeleton + custom Airflow image next (authorable now), or is this a good place to pause?
